findings.claim: This diff REMOVES barriers rather than introducing them, so it does not create a new perf regression of the historical 'redundant-collective'/'defensive-oversync' type. Specifically: (1) in checkpointing.py save_checkpoint(), the unconditional post-schedule `torch.distributed.barrier()` (whose own comment already said 'not necessary') is removed and replaced by a barrier gated on `not args.async_save`, so the async path now avoids a full-world sync that previously serialized every rank after scheduling an async save. (2) In training.py save_checkpoint_and_time(), the `timers(timer_key, ...).start(barrier=True)` and `.stop(barrier=True)` were changed to `barrier=False`, eliminating two extra full-world barriers per checkpoint save; the code comments correctly note that timer.log() reports min & max so a start/stop barrier is not needed for correct measurement. This matches the *fix* side of the 'redundant-collective'/'defensive-oversync' patterns, not an introduction of one. No new blocking-d2h-sync or added collectives are present in the diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-iteration checkpoint save wall time and DP-group barrier stall (async_save on/off) before vs after this commit; confirm removal of the 2 timer barriers + async-path barrier does not skew reported save-checkpoint timer min/max.

The new `_NCCLEPManager.get_permuted_hidden_states_by_experts` (dynamic-shape path, static_shape=False) computes `num_valid = int(self.tokens_per_expert.sum().item())` on the hot forward path. `.item()` forces a device-to-host sync that stalls the CPU on the GPU comm/compute stream, serializing dispatch against the expert GEMM and exposing communication that would otherwise be overlapped. This is exactly the D2H-sync class the sibling HybridEP backend was designed to avoid: HybridEP takes an explicit `num_permuted_tokens` precisely because 'HybridEP obtains the size from a GPU tensor, which causes a D2H synchronization.' The NCCL EP path re-introduces that sync via `.item()`.

findings.claim: `unshard_parameters()` and `reduce_gradients()` are on the per-step (per-microbatch) hot path (called from module.py's forward/backward hooks). The new code calls `self._unsharded_model_weight.rendezvous(gather_axis)` inside `unshard_parameters()` and `partial_grad.rendezvous(reduce_axis)` inside `reduce_gradients()` on EVERY invocation whenever `_symm_mem_pool is not None`. `symm_mem.rendezvous()` is a collective/registration-style handshake that should only need to happen once per persistent buffer. For `_unsharded_model_weight` (a persistent buffer whose storage is only resized, not reallocated to a new address, via `_resize_storage`), re-running rendezvous on every unshard is redundant duplicate work that can be cached/registered once. This repeated per-step rendezvous adds collective/CPU overhead on the critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile per-step forward/backward with use_symm_mem=True vs False; count symm_mem.rendezvous invocations and measure host-side + collective overhead per training step across many steps.

findings.claim: The `partial_grad` DBuffer in `reduce_gradients()` is freshly created each call via `DBuffer.distribute_tensors(...)` under the symmetric-memory MemPool context, so its local_buffer is a new allocation each step; calling `rendezvous()` on it every step is inherent to that per-step allocation pattern. This means the symmetric-memory staging buffer for gradients is not persistent/reused, incurring a rendezvous handshake per reduce. Consider a persistent reduce-scatter staging buffer registered once, matching how PyTorch's symmetric-memory user-buffer allocators cache registration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure allocation + rendezvous cost of the per-step partial_grad symmetric buffer vs a persistent pre-registered reduce-scatter buffer.

findings.claim: 当 --mamba-training-ssm-states-dtype 未设置时（默认 None），MambaMixer.__init__ 用 `self.mamba_training_ssm_states_dtype = config.mamba_training_ssm_states_dtype or config.params_dtype` 将其兜底为 params_dtype，然后在热路径 forward 中当 MAMBA_HAS_STATE_DTYPE 为真时始终通过 `state_dtype_kwarg={'state_dtype': self.mamba_training_ssm_states_dtype}` 显式传给 mamba_split_conv1d_scan_combined / mamba_chunk_scan_combined。这与文档字符串所述『None causes the states to follow the activation dtype』不一致：代码传的是 params_dtype 而非激活 dtype，且是显式覆盖 kernel 的默认 state_dtype（通常内部为 fp32 以保证 inter-chunk 状态数值精度）。对于 bf16 训练（params_dtype=bf16）这会在用户没有主动开启该 flag 时静默把 inter-chunk SSM 状态从 fp32 降到 bf16——这是一个默认路径的行为改变。虽然 bf16 状态可减少内存/带宽（可能提升吞吐），但更值得关注的是它把一个『需要用户显式选择的低精度优化』变成了默认行为，可能带来收敛/数值退化，属于 config/默认值静默选择路径的模式。建议：flag 为 None 时应传空 kwarg（沿用 kernel fp32 默认），而不是兜底成 params_dtype 再显式传入。
findings.suggested_benchmark: bf16 训练下对比 flag=None（默认，本 PR 后传 state_dtype=bf16）与旧行为（kernel fp32 默认）的每步吞吐、显存占用及 loss 收敛曲线

This revert re-introduces an unconditional (non-opt-in) `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` on every checkpoint save inside `save_checkpoint_and_time`. `empty_cache()` forces a full device sync and returns all cached free blocks to the CUDA driver, so on the *next* iteration after the checkpoint the caching allocator must re-request memory from the driver via cudaMalloc (expensive, serializes). Because it runs every time a checkpoint is taken (a cold-ish but recurring path), it introduces a per-checkpoint stall + subsequent allocator re-warm cost. Note: unlike the historical `--empty-unused-memory-each-iter` case which is a gated knob (default off), here the call is always executed with no flag guard.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure iteration time immediately before vs. after a checkpoint save (with async checkpointing enabled); compare with the empty_cache()/free_overlap_buffers() lines removed to isolate the allocator re-warm + sync cost.

Combined with get_rl_parallel_generation_tasks() now multiplying parallel_generation_tasks by grpo_prompts_per_step (and again by grpo_group_size for granularity 'R'), the number of in-flight generation coroutines / concurrent agenerate() calls can grow multiplicatively versus the previous formula. If not otherwise gated, this increases the effective inference concurrency and the httpx connection concurrency_limit (megatron/rl/inference/megatron.py) proportionally, which can oversubscribe the inference engine and degrade throughput/latency for the default 'B' path. Verify that submission_gate still bounds true concurrency in the 'B'/'G' paths since the gate is now sized to parallel_generation_tasks rather than num_workers.

This diff FIXES (not introduces) the known scratch-buffer OOM. In parent, MambaSlotAllocator sizes `intermediate_ssm_out`/`intermediate_conv_out` as `MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST(=3) * max_requests` slots (mamba_slot_allocator.py:104), but `_allocate_mamba_cache` sized the durable cache as `total_bytes // per_slot_bytes` WITHOUT reserving the scratch. Total GPU usage silently exceeded prefix_caching_mamba_gb (matched historical case 11: 98 GiB scratch at max_requests=256). The diff now reserves scratch_bytes = 3*max_requests*per_slot_bytes up front, computes durable max_slots from the remainder, and raises ValueError if the budget cannot fit scratch + one durable slot. No new regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Allocate MambaSlotAllocator with default (uncapped) max_requests auto-derived from a large buffer_size_gb; assert peak GPU bytes for intermediate_* buffers + durable cache stays within prefix_caching_mamba_gb.

findings.claim: The new `_get_or_create_bridge_pg` calls `dist.new_group(ranks, backend='nccl')` from inside `BridgeCommunicator.__init__` (invoked at line ~168 via `self.bridge_pg = self._get_or_create_bridge_pg(bridge_ranks)`). `torch.distributed.new_group` is a COLLECTIVE that must be entered by EVERY rank of the default/world group with the SAME arguments, even ranks that will not be members. In this multi-module bridge setup, `BridgeCommunicator` is constructed for each (src_grid, dest_grid) pair, and the bridge PG only spans the union of tp-leader ranks (`sorted(set(src_tp_leaders) | set(dest_tp_leaders))`). If any world rank does not construct this BridgeCommunicator, or constructs a different set of bridges in a different order (e.g. ranks belonging only to unrelated grids/modules), the `new_group` calls will be mismatched across ranks and NCCL group creation will deadlock/hang — exactly the failure mode of the retrieved `mismatched-collective-config` cases (new_group / broadcast called with a non-uniform group membership across ranks). Note the existing broadcast-PG path uses `dist.new_subgroups_by_enumeration(...)` with the FULL list of rank sublists (so every rank passes the same enumeration), whereas the new bridge path passes only one rank subset, making it not obviously collective-safe.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-module (>=3 modules / >2 grids) pipeline init test where some world ranks belong to grids not involved in a given bridge; assert BridgeCommunicator construction (and thus new_group) completes without hang across all ranks.

Default distribution flips from LINEAR (dense small/mid coverage: [1,2,4]+range(8,256,8)+range(256,max,16)) to EXPONENTIAL (log-spaced halving) and CUDA_GRAPH_ROUNDER drops 8->2. EXPONENTIAL gives ~2x worst-case token padding at every scale, but real batches whose token_count falls between two log-spaced graphs now pad up to the next larger captured graph, and the smallest sizes (1,2,4...) present in LINEAR are only partially reconstructed (auto-size decode floor adds lcm(spec_unit,tp) * {1,2}). If typical serving token counts cluster in the mid range that LINEAR covered densely, decode/prefill steps may run slightly larger padded shapes than before, increasing per-step compute. This matches the historical 'captured batch sizes affect per-step latency' pattern; verify measured decode latency is not regressed for the target workload's request-size distribution.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Replay a representative request-size trace and measure average padded token_count and per-step decode/prefill latency under EXPONENTIAL vs LINEAR distributions.

findings.claim: This PR repurposes the existing `cuda_graph_use_single_mempool` config flag (previously scoped to the `local` cuda_graph implementation in cuda_graphs.py, where it controls whether captured graphs share one mempool and thus may only be used once per step) but simultaneously flips its DEFAULT from False to True in transformer_config.py:894. Because the SAME flag is still read by the `local` implementation (CudaGraphManager.global_mempool, cuda_graphs.py:1413), flipping the default silently changes behavior for existing `local` cuda-graph users who never opted in: their per-microbatch cudagraphs will now be forced into a single mempool, which the old docstring explicitly warned 'may greatly increase the number of cudagraphs created when the number of microbatches is high.' This is a config-default regression that alters memory/graph-count behavior of an unrelated code path.

findings.claim: This PR flips the MSC feature flag from default-enabled to default-disabled (`MultiStorageClientFeature(_msc_available)` → `_FeatureFlag(default=False)`, and CLI `--disable-msc`(default enable) → `--enable-msc`(default False)). However, per the docs, MSC on regular filesystem paths is 'a very thin abstraction layer with negligible performance impact', and disabling it actually RESTORES the CachedMetadataFileSystemReader path (fast, metadata cached). So this default flip is not a throughput/latency regression on the hot path — if anything it re-enables metadata caching. This matches the config-toggle mechanism (a static default value routes to a different code path) but the direction here is neutral-to-positive for perf, so no regression is reported as such.
findings.suggested_benchmark: dist-checkpoint save/load throughput with and without --enable-msc on a regular filesystem path

findings.claim: In NVLSAllGatherVDispatcher.dispatch_preprocess, the shared-expert forward is launched on the side stream SharedExpertMLP.stream reading `hidden_states` directly (`apply_module(self.shared_experts)(hidden_states)`), while the main stream continues to consume/reshape the same `hidden_states` (view -> AGV dispatch, expert GEMMs, RSV). Only `stream.wait_stream(current_stream())` is issued (side stream waits for producer). There is NO `record_stream` on `hidden_states` nor a clone. If `hidden_states` was allocated by the caching allocator and its last main-stream use finishes before the side-stream shared-expert GEMMs read it, the allocator can free/reuse the buffer while the side stream is still reading — the exact race mechanism flagged in the historical shared-experts overlap case (clone-in-side-stream-without-record_stream). Add `hidden_states.record_stream(SharedExpertMLP.stream)` (or keep a reference alive until combine_postprocess).
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE NVLS inference decode/prefill with --moe-shared-expert-overlap under memory pressure; run with PYTORCH_NO_CUDA_MEMORY_CACHING / compute-sanitizer to surface cross-stream use-after-free

For workloads where max_tokens >> max_requests, prefill/mixed steps that previously had a matching captured graph may now fall outside CG coverage and run eager, missing the CG speedup (opportunity-not-turned-on). The functional test's removal of `--...-cuda-graph-max-tokens: 2048` is consistent with this default-behavior shift and should be perf-validated.

findings.claim: In _compute_per_buffer_param_layout, the shared-embedding handling (step 3) emits one isolated bucket per shared-embedding param and, to keep the 'no param crosses a shard boundary' invariant, fills shards 1..dp_size-1 with empty padding slots sized to the full embedding numel. As the code's own NOTE admits, this costs (dp_size-1) * pad_to_divisor(numel, shard_divisor) elements per shared embedding — e.g. a 128k x 8192 tied embedding at dp_size=8 is ~7 * (vocab*hidden) elements of dead padding in BOTH the param buffer and grad buffer (many GB). This is a buffer layout that over-allocates the DDP buffers versus the legacy ping-pong shard_params path (which did not allocate contiguous DP-sized buffers per param). This is a real buffer-sizing regression for models with shared/tied embeddings.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure param+grad buffer peak memory for a GPT model with tied input/output embeddings (large vocab) under LayerWiseDistributedOptimizer at dp_size>=8 with the new layout vs legacy path.

vllm_fused_moe.py removes @triton.autotune over _AUTOTUNE_CONFIGS and replaces it with a static host-side heuristic _get_default_config(M,E,top_k). The tile/warp/stage parameters are now derived only from M (num_tokens_hint), E, and top_k — NOT keyed on N/K/device_name. This is exactly the historically risky pattern: a hardcoded default config that ignores the intermediate size N/K and the actual GPU. On GPUs with smaller shared memory (e.g. L4) the large-M branch (BLOCK_SIZE_M=128, BLOCK_SIZE_N=128, BLOCK_SIZE_K=64, num_warps=8, num_stages=3) can exceed available SMEM or land far from optimal for a given (N,K), causing latency/throughput regression vs the prior autotuned selection. Prior code autotuned over 24 configs keyed on ['N','K'], so it adapted to the FC1 (N large) vs FC2 (K large) shapes; the new single config is shared for both FC1 and FC2, which can be suboptimal for one of the two GEMMs.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark vllm_fused_moe FC1+FC2 latency across decode (M small) and prefill (M large) on H100 and a smaller-SMEM GPU (L4/A10), comparing the removed autotune path vs the new _get_default_config heuristic for representative (E,N,K).

`SymmetricMemoryManager._default_size_mb` was bumped from 256 to 512. The 'tp' symmetric-memory buffer (allocated via `SymmetricMemoryManager.get_buffer("tp", process_group=self.tp_group)` in inference_layers.py, which never passes an explicit `size_mb`) now falls back to 512 MB of symmetric memory per rank instead of 256 MB. This doubles the persistent symmetric-memory (NVLink/multimem) footprint for all TP inference paths that touch the 'tp' buffer, even when the actual all-gather / reduce-scatter tensors are far smaller. This is a resident-capacity increase, not a hot-path allocation, so latency is unaffected, but per-rank device memory reserved for symmetric memory doubles. If the intent was only to enlarge the EP dispatcher buffers (which now self-size via explicit size_mb), the default bump is an unnecessary global side effect on the 'tp' key.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-rank CUDA symmetric-memory reservation (torch.cuda memory / symm_mem.empty numel) for a TP>1 inference_optimized run before/after; confirm 'tp' buffer grows 256MB->512MB and that this is intended vs. only EP buffers needing growth.

findings.claim: The default for `moe_hybridep_num_sms` changed from a hard-coded, explicitly-tuned `16` (docstring: 'In pure NVL scenarios, 16 SMs can generally achieve good bandwidth') to `None`. In `init_hybrid_ep_buffer`, a `None` value causes the `num_sms_dispatch_api`/`num_sms_combine_api` kwargs to be *omitted* entirely from the `HybridEPBuffer(...)` constructor (they are only added to `kwargs` when not None), so the buffer now falls back to DeepEP's internal default rather than 16. The parent code's `HybridEPDispatch.forward` shows the fallback default was `24`, so the actual number of SMs allocated for HybridEP dispatch/combine communication on the hot MoE path silently changes (likely 16 -> 24). This alters the tuned comm/compute SM balance on every dispatch/combine and can regress bandwidth/throughput for users relying on the prior default, without any explicit opt-in.
findings.suggested_benchmark: HybridEP MoE dispatch+combine microbenchmark on pure-NVL topology comparing num_sms=16 (old default) vs new None/DeepEP-default, measuring all-to-all bandwidth and end-to-end MoE layer latency.

The MTP CUDA-graph warmup in dynamic_engine.py only captures graphs for the *derived* MTP batch sizes `n` seen during the decoder warmup loop (deduped via `mtp_seen_batch_sizes`). The MTP batch size is computed as `n = cuda_graph_batch_dimension.req_count` (optionally rounded up for SP), which is a different granularity than the decoder token-count graphs. If, at runtime, an MTP step is invoked with a `req_count` (or SP-rounded value) that was never produced by the warmup batch-dimension list — e.g. because `is_valid` now rejects `token_count<=0` dims or because decode-only token counts collapse to a subset of req_counts — the MTP path will not have a matching captured graph and will fall back to eager / trigger a fresh capture on the hot decode path, causing per-step CUDA-graph capture stalls. This is the classic warmup-shape-gap mechanism: compile/warmup config is fine but warmup misses shapes hit at runtime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Dynamic-batching MTP inference: sweep decode batch sizes (req_count) that are NOT on the CUDA-graph batch-dimension list and measure per-step latency / CUDA-graph re-capture events for the MTP head.

findings.claim: In `_get_decode_A_neg_exp`, the fast (cached) inference path is gated on `not (self.training or torch.is_grad_enabled())`. This mirrors the historical `cudagraph-enablement` boundary case: frozen/inference layers wrapped by CudaGraphManager can have `self.training==True` while `torch.is_grad_enabled()==False` (RL train/eval or frozen layers). During such graph capture, the `if self.training or torch.is_grad_enabled():` branch is taken, which recomputes `-torch.exp(self.A_log.float())` and builds a fresh expand view every token, defeating the entire purpose of the cache and re-introducing the 3 small elementwise kernels the change set out to eliminate. The intended fast path is only reached when both are False. If any inference/eval scenario keeps `self.training==True`, the cache never helps.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Mamba decode-only microbench with model.train() left True but torch.no_grad(): measure kernel launch count / decode step latency vs model.eval().

The diff adds a FlashAttention-4 (`flash_attn.cute.flash_attn_varlen_func`) backend path in `flash_decode_and_prefill`, gated by `HAVE_FA4` and preferred over FA3/FA2 (prefill branch ~line 838, decode branch ~line 919). This is a specialized-backend swap of the same nature as the historically-tracked FA3 (Hopper) inference path addition. It is opt-in via import availability (`HAVE_FA4` only True if `flash_attn.cute` imports), so it does not force a slow path by default. No regression is introduced for existing FA3/FA2 users since those branches are preserved as `elif`. Flagged only as informational: the new path's `num_splits=1` is hardcoded (vs the FA2/FA3 path's `num_splits=0` auto-heuristic), which could underutilize SMs / reduce decode throughput on large KV caches compared to letting the kernel auto-split.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Dynamic-batching decode throughput (tokens/s) with FA4 available vs FA3, at large KV-cache/context lengths, comparing num_splits=1 vs auto (0).

The new DeepStream backend's `decode_indices` decodes the ENTIRE video stream regardless of how few frames are requested. The docstring itself states "The whole stream is decoded; the pool keeps exactly the frames whose decode-order index is in `frame_indices` ... and sends EOS once the last one is matched." This is the exact anti-pattern the historical cases fixed for the OpenCV/PyAV paths: instead of seeking, the pipeline runs NVDEC over every frame up to the last requested index. For uniform sampling of a long video (e.g. 8 frames from a 10-minute clip) this decodes tens of thousands of frames vs the handful actually needed. Note the sibling PyAV backend (PyAVVideoBackendMixin.decode_frames) explicitly seeks per-frame to avoid re-decoding, and PyNvVideoCodec uses a SimpleDecoder with indexed access; the DeepStream path forgoes both. EOS is only sent after the last target index is matched, so decode cost scales with `max(frame_indices)` (~video length) rather than `len(frame_indices)`.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode 8 uniformly-sampled frames from a 10-min 1080p H264 clip via backend=deepstream vs pynvvideocodec/pyav; compare wall time and NVDEC frame count. Vary video length to confirm deepstream latency scales with total frames while others stay flat.

MiniMaxM3IndexerMSAImpl.forward now resets a huge unified score buffer to -inf on every forward via `unified_scores.fill_(_SCORE_SENTINEL)` in build() over the view `unified_scores_buffer[:num_tokens]` shaped `[num_tokens, num_index_heads, MAX_K_TILES=8192]` fp32. For num_index_heads=4 and even a modest num_tokens, this touches num_tokens*4*8192*4 bytes per forward (e.g. 512 tokens -> ~64 MB written every layer's build). MAX_K_TILES=8192 is a fixed capture-time constant (1M-token context) regardless of the actual sequence length, so short/typical requests pay the full -inf memset over 8192 tiles even when only a few tiles are causally valid. The old path never materialized such a buffer: decode's `minimax_m3_index_decode` allocated `[H, total_q, round_up(max_block,16)]` sized to the real max_block, and prefill's fmha wrote its native max_k_tiles. This is a redundant fixed-size zero/fill on an over-sized buffer whose valid region is far smaller than 8192.

findings.claim: This diff FIXES a cudagraph key-mismatch bug rather than introducing a regression. In the DBO/microbatching path, the cudagraph store key in `_capture_ubatches` was computed as `ubatch_metadata[0].num_tokens + ubatch_metadata[1].num_tokens` (first two ubatches only), while the lookup key in `__call__` uses `num_tokens = sum(ubatch_slice.num_tokens for ubatch_slice in ubatch_slices)` (all ubatches). When `parallel_config.num_ubatches > 2`, the store key and lookup key diverge: the graph is stored under a smaller key than it is looked up under. This causes the lookup at line ~516 (`self.cudagraphs[num_tokens]`) to miss, so the FULL-cudagraph fast path is never taken and the model re-captures / falls back to eager microbatched execution every step (repeated per-iteration work + missed cudagraph replay = latency regression), matching the historical DBO cudagraph BatchDescriptor/num_tokens mismatch mechanism. The change to `sum(m.num_tokens for m in ubatch_metadata)` aligns store and lookup keys. No new regression is introduced by the diff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run DBO / dual-batch-overlap with parallel_config.num_ubatches>2 and CUDAGraphMode.FULL; assert self.cudagraphs lookup hits (no re-capture per step) and measure per-step latency vs num_ubatches==2.

findings.claim: In FlashInferB12xExperts.apply(), the parent passed the pre-allocated destination `output` tensor directly into `flashinfer_b12x_fused_moe(..., output=output)`, so the fused kernel wrote its result in place with no extra copy. The new code calls `wrapper_output = wrapper.run(...)` (which allocates a fresh output tensor internally) and then does `output.copy_(wrapper_output)`. This introduces, on every MoE forward (hot path), (1) an extra full-size [M,K] device output allocation inside the wrapper and (2) an extra device-to-device copy of that tensor into `output`. For large models with many MoE layers per step this is measurable extra memory-bandwidth and allocation churn compared to the previous alloc-free in-place write. If B12xMoEWrapper.run supports an out= / output= argument, pass `output` directly to eliminate the copy.
findings.suggested_benchmark: MoE decode throughput/latency on a B12x (SM120) device (e.g. Nemotron-H or an NVFP4 MoE), comparing in-place kernel output vs wrapper.run + output.copy_()

findings.claim: `numba_workqueue_threading_layer()` in `KimiK25FusedVisionProcessor.preprocess` is entered on every preprocess() invocation (per-request multimodal hot path), and inside it mutates process-global state twice: `os.environ['NUMBA_THREADING_LAYER']='workqueue'` and `numba.config.THREADING_LAYER='workqueue'`, then restores them in `finally`. `import numba` is also re-executed each call (cheap after first, but still). The context-manager save/restore of global config on a per-item basis is init-shaped work placed on the hot path; the docstring itself notes the threading-layer choice is 'sticky for the life of the process once launched', so re-applying it every preprocess() call yields no payoff after the first call and only adds per-request overhead / global-state churn.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark KimiK25FusedVisionProcessor.preprocess() throughput with many small images vs. hoisting the threading-layer setup to one-time process init.

findings.claim: In flashinfer_autotune(), the guard that disables the persistent autotune cache was broadened from `all2all_backend in {deepep_high_throughput, deepep_low_latency, deepep_v2}` to `get_world_group().world_size > 1`. This routes ALL multi-rank distributed deployments (any TP/PP/EP>1, not just DeepEP backends) onto the non-persistent path. On that path every rank runs a full autotuning `_dummy_run` (instead of only rank 0 tuning and broadcasting the cache), and the tuned tactics are never written to / reloaded from the on-disk cache, so each restart re-tunes from scratch. This increases model warmup/startup time for the common multi-GPU case that previously used the cached leader-only path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure kernel_warmup/flashinfer_autotune wall time on a TP>1 non-DeepEP config across a cold start and a warm restart, comparing world_size>1 (new) vs the previous DeepEP-only gate to quantify added tuning time and lost cache reuse.

In DeepEPHTPrepareAndFinalize._sync_dbo_comm_if_needed(), a full host-blocking torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize() is inserted after every DeepEP HT dispatch (line ~173) and after every combine (line ~389) whenever running on ROCm with DBO enabled. DBO's whole purpose (see ubatching.py: separate comm_stream/compute_stream and per-ubatch gpu_comm/compute_done events, and the comment in _do_dispatch: 'yield before launching the dispatch kernel ... so we want to queue up all the compute for the other ubatch before the dispatch kernel starts') is to overlap one ubatch's all2all collective with the other ubatch's compute. A blocking current_stream().synchronize() drains the stream and blocks the CPU, forcing the collective to complete before any other work is queued — serializing comm against compute and reintroducing the exact comm bubbles DBO was built to hide. This matches the historical missing-comm-overlap pattern (symptom: gpu-util-or-bubble). It is guarded to ROCm+enable_dbo+deepep_high_throughput and is motivated by a correctness fix (workspace reuse across ubatches corrupting DP+EP accuracy), so the throughput loss is a deliberate correctness trade-off on that path rather than a general regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: ROCm DP+EP throughput/decode-latency with --all2all-backend deepep_high_throughput and DBO enabled, comparing tokens/s and GPU util before vs after this commit

findings.claim: In the weightless RMSNorm path on XPU (has_weight=False, weight is None), the diff replaces the native no-multiply path with allocating a fresh `torch.ones(x.shape[-1], device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)` tensor on every forward call, then invoking `torch.ops._C.rms_norm(...)`. RMSNorm.forward is a per-layer, per-token hot path (executed once or twice per transformer layer per forward step), so this materializes and fills a device buffer of size hidden_size on the device on every invocation. torch.ones triggers a fill kernel launch each call; the ones tensor is functionally a no-op multiply that the previous native path skipped entirely. This is wasted per-call buffer allocation + fill work on a hot path. Since weight is a fixed all-ones vector, it should be cached once (or the weightless kernel path used) rather than reallocated each forward. Same pattern applied to both rms_norm and fused_add_rms_norm.

The diff adds Mxfp4MoeBackend.EMULATION to the tail of the auto-selection priority list in `_get_priority_backends_for_gpt_oss()`. The emulation backend (OCP_MXQuantizationEmulationTritonExperts) dequantizes MoE weights on the fly every forward and runs BF16 TritonExperts — its own constructor warns it 'may be slower than native quantized MOE'. Because the auto `select_mxfp4_moe_backend` loop returns the FIRST backend whose `is_supported_config` returns True, and emulation now sits in the fallback chain, on hardware where the faster native backends (TRTLLM / AITER / TRITON / CUTLASS / MARLIN / XPU) all fail `is_supported_config`, the auto path will now silently land on the slow emulation kernel instead of raising. Previously such configs raised NotImplementedError forcing the user to explicitly opt in via `--moe-backend emulation` (the removed help text confirms this was intentional opt-in per PR #45896). This is a classic fast-kernel-exists-but-gating-now-routes-to-slow-path regression: MI300/MI250 gpt-oss/quark runs that used to require explicit opt-in will now auto-select emulation, and the removed test overrides (`model_args['moe_backend']='emulation'`) show these very configs now rely on the auto path picking emulation implicitly.

findings.claim: The change moves cudaHostRegister pinning off the init critical path into a background thread (CPUTensorPinThread) so worker startup no longer blocks on cudaHostRegister of potentially many GB. This is a startup-latency improvement. However, transfer_async/submit_store/submit_load can now begin before pin_thread completes (there is no join or is_pinned check on the transfer hot path). During the window after startup but before pinning finishes, GPU<->CPU transfers execute against unpinned host memory (unpinned DMA). This is a transient (bounded) slowdown, not a correctness bug, but early offload/load transfers may be slower until the background thread finishes. Consider gating the first transfer on pin_thread completion or documenting that early transfers may be unpinned.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure H2D/D2H bandwidth for the first KV offload transfers issued immediately after worker init vs after pin_thread.join(), comparing pinned vs unpinned DMA throughput.

findings.claim: In FlashAttnMLASparseImpl.forward_mqa (the decode hot path, executed once per attention layer per forward step), a new GPU tensor is allocated on every call: `cu_seqlens_q = torch.arange(0, num_actual_toks + 1, dtype=torch.int32, device=q_rope.device)`. Since this backend is decode-only with reorder_batch_threshold=1 and query length is always 1, cu_seqlens_q is simply [0,1,2,...,num_actual_toks] — a value that depends only on batch size and can be precomputed once into a persistent buffer in the MetadataBuilder (as done for req_id_per_token_buffer, and as rocm_aiter_mla_sparse does with self.qo_indptr = torch.arange(...) in __init__). The sibling FlashInferMLASparseImpl and flashattn_mla.py reuse the precomputed attn_metadata.query_start_loc instead of allocating per-forward. This repeated device-side arange launch + allocation per layer per step adds avoidable kernel-launch/alloc overhead on the decode critical path (and can also break/weaken CUDA-graph capture friendliness).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-only throughput/latency benchmark on Hopper with a DeepSeek-style sparse MLA model (index_topk set) using FLASH_ATTN_MLA_SPARSE; measure per-step decode latency and compare against precomputing cu_seqlens_q in the builder vs per-forward torch.arange.

check_moe_marlin_supports_layer now returns True for tile-misaligned MoE intermediate sizes (allow_tile_padding=True), causing those layers to zero-pad the intermediate dimension up to the next multiple of lcm(64, group_size). At weight prep this is one-time work, but the padded intermediate persists into every forward: fused_marlin_moe reads N via marlin_moe_intermediate_size(w1, w2) from the *padded* weight shapes, so gate-up produces w13_num_shards*padded_N columns and the down GEMM consumes padded_N (size_k=N), i.e. every MoE forward runs slightly larger GEMMs plus larger intermediate_cache allocations. The code itself acknowledges this ('Padded experts pad/slice activations on every forward; performance may be degraded'). For sizes needing significant padding (e.g. N=96 -> 128, ~33% extra intermediate work) this is a per-forward throughput cost that did not exist on the previous WNA16 fallback path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Fused MoE throughput for a WNA16/FP8 MoE model with a tile-misaligned intermediate_size_per_partition (e.g. N=96, 160) comparing padded-Marlin vs. the prior moe_wna16 Triton fallback, tokens/s at decode and prefill.

This diff is a PERFORMANCE FIX (not a regression) for a redundant-collective pattern. Before, MergedColumnParallelLinearWithLoRA.apply (the NON-fully-sharded merged-column LoRA class) called _mcp_apply(x, bias, self), and _mcp_apply issues an UNCONDITIONAL tensor_model_parallel_all_gather(buffers) on the LoRA-A shrink output. That all_gather is only correct/needed for the S-LoRA fully-sharded scheme (where the rank dim is sharded across TP ranks). On the non-fully-sharded path the LoRA A rank is replicated (full rank stored in lora_a_stacked), so the all_gather was an extra, unnecessary TP collective on the hot per-layer LoRA apply path — a classic redundant-collective causing gpu-util-or-bubble / throughput loss under TP>1. The change routes this class to super().apply() -> BaseLinearLayerWithLoRA._apply_sync -> add_lora_linear (no all_gather), and the new assert `layer.lora_config.fully_sharded_loras` in _mcp_apply now guards that path to only the fully-sharded scheme. Fully-sharded subclasses (…WithShardedLoRA) still call _mcp_apply and keep their needed all_gather. Net effect: eliminates a redundant all_gather per merged-column LoRA layer in the default (non-fully-sharded) TP path. No regression introduced.
findings.suggested_benchmark: LoRA decode/prefill throughput with a MergedColumnParallelLinear (e.g. gate_up_proj) adapter under TP>1 (e.g. TP=2/4), fully_sharded_loras=False, comparing tokens/s and per-layer collective count before vs after.

findings.claim: In the do_expand=False (decode/cudagraph) path, the new code passes num_max_tokens_per_rank = 1 << max(n-1,0).bit_length(), where n = max(num_tokens_across_dp_cpu). Since the comment itself documents that DeepEP JIT-compiles a separate dispatch kernel per distinct num_max_tokens_per_rank, this makes the compiled-kernel set depend on the runtime DP-padded batch size. In the parent code no num_max_tokens_per_rank was passed, so the buffer's single fixed init value (max_num_batched_tokens) was used and the dispatch kernel compiled exactly once. The new scheme trades that single-shape guarantee for up to ~log2(max_num_batched_tokens) power-of-2 shapes. If any of those power-of-2 sizes are not touched during cudagraph capture / warmup, the FIRST decode iteration that hits a new batch size will trigger an on-the-path DeepEP JIT compile stall (a 'cicc storm'), i.e. a runtime latency spike under changing concurrency — exactly the warmup-shape-gap mechanism. This is only safe if warmup exercises every power-of-2 batch size that decode can produce; otherwise it is a latency regression on cold shapes.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-mode DeepEP V2 (use_cudagraph=True) throughput/latency sweep across a range of DP-padded batch sizes that cross multiple power-of-2 boundaries (e.g. 1,2,3,...,64,...,4096) under high DP concurrency; measure per-iteration latency to detect JIT-compile stalls on first hit of each new num_max_tokens_per_rank bucket, comparing against parent (fixed single-shape) baseline.

findings.claim: The new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is prepended to _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[CUDA] ahead of FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel, so on SM10x it becomes the default auto-selected NVFP4 linear backend. Unlike the CUTLASS kernel, it does NOT override input_quant_key() (base returns None) and unconditionally calls scaled_fp4_quant() inside apply_weights(). The CUTLASS kernel exposes input_quant_key()==kNvfp4Dynamic, which via expose_input_quant_key() lets the quant-activation fusion pass fold the activation quantization into an upstream op and pass a pre-quantized QuantizedActivation (the qa branch), skipping the in-kernel scaled_fp4_quant. Because the CuteDSL kernel lacks this hook, whenever it is auto-selected the fusion no longer applies and the input quantization runs as a separate kernel on the hot path per linear call, losing the fusion the CUTLASS path enjoyed. This matches the historical kernel-fusion regression where removing/bypassing a fused input-quantization step re-introduces a standalone quantize kernel.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NVFP4 (w4a4) model decode latency on SM100 with --linear-backend auto vs flashinfer_cutlass, comparing per-layer quant+GEMM cost; verify whether activation quant is fused for cutlass but standalone for cutedsl

findings.claim: Auto-selection default for NVFP4 linear layers on CUDA/SM10x changes from FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel to FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel by inserting the new kernel at the head of _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA]. is_supported() gates only on is_device_capability_family(100) + has_flashinfer() and can_implement() always returns True, so on all SM100-family devices this silently swaps the default GEMM implementation. If the cutedsl kernel is not universally faster than cutlass across shapes (small M / decode vs large prefill), this is a fused-backend-swap that can regress throughput/latency for the default configuration, mirroring the historical fused-backend-swap where the FlashInfer default backend switch changed performance characteristics. Recommend confirming cutedsl wins across representative shapes before making it the auto default, or keeping cutlass first.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep NVFP4 GEMM shapes (M in {1,8,64,512,4096}, typical N/K) on SM100 comparing flashinfer cute-dsl vs cutlass backend GEMM latency to confirm auto-default choice is not a regression

In the new Triton kernel `_fused_inv_rope_fp8_quant_per_head`, the PDL primitives are issued in the wrong order and wrong place: `tl.extra.cuda.gdc_launch_dependents()` is called BEFORE `tl.extra.cuda.gdc_wait()`, and both are placed at the very TOP of the kernel (before any input load / rope math / output store). The correct PDL discipline (which the sibling CUDA kernel `per_token_group_quant_8bit_packed_register_kernel` in the same PR implements correctly) is: `griddepcontrol.wait` at the START (block until the prior producer kernel's global-memory writes are visible before consuming input), then `griddepcontrol.launch_dependents` at the END, only AFTER this kernel has finished writing its outputs, so the dependent (consumer) kernel can begin. Signaling launch_dependents at kernel entry (before o_ptr loads and before fp8/scale stores) lets the dependent kernel start prematurely and defeats the producer->consumer overlap the PDL feature is meant to provide, i.e. the overlap is nullified rather than gained. This matches the historical `dma-access-order-relax` mechanism where the ordering of a GPU synchronization primitive relative to the memory it guards is what determines correctness/overlap.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench the DeepseekV4 attention fused_inv_rope_fp8_quant path (o -> fp8+scale) on SM90/SM100 with USE_GDC on vs off, and compare against the intended wait-first/launch-last ordering; measure end-to-end decode throughput to confirm PDL overlap is actually realized rather than nullified.

Removing AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer replaces micro-batched tokenizer encode/decode with per-request blocking calls submitted individually to make_async(self._encode/_decode, executor=self._executor). AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer previously coalesced encode/decode requests arriving within a ~2ms window (up to max_batch_size=32) into a single batched tokenizer call (self.tokenizer(prompts, ...) / tokenizer.batch_decode(...)), amortizing per-call Python/tokenizer overhead across concurrent requests. The new code issues one tokenizer.encode()/decode() call per request. Because the shared self._executor is a ThreadPoolExecutor sized by renderer_num_workers (default = 1), under concurrent front-end load these individual tokenizer calls are serialized one-at-a-time through a single worker thread with no batching. This is the exact inverse of the historical commit that ADDED AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer to fix serial per-request tokenization; the throughput benefit under concurrency is reverted, matching the serial-io prior (bottleneck is serialization of tokenization itself, not the storage path).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark OpenAI front-end tokenization throughput/TTFT with many concurrent requests (e.g. high-concurrency completions/chat) on a single-worker renderer (renderer_num_workers=1), comparing parent (batched AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer) vs this commit (per-request make_async encode/decode).

findings.claim: `get_prefill_chunk_plan` is invoked inside `_forward_prefill` (vllm/models/deepseek_v4/nvidia/flashmla.py), which is called once per DeepseekV4 attention layer (~60 layers) on every prefill step. The chunk plan it computes depends only on shared `swa_metadata` fields (prefill_seq_lens_cpu, prefill_query_lens_cpu, window_size, max_model_len, max_num_batched_tokens) plus per-class constants (compress_ratio, PREFILL_CHUNK_SIZE), i.e. it is identical for all layers of the same type within a step. The old code derived N/M/num_chunks from cheap arithmetic; the new code runs a Python host-side loop (see below) that is now re-executed identically per layer. The plan should be computed once per build() and cached on the metadata object (like tile_sched_*), not recomputed in the per-layer forward path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSv4 prefill with many layers + moderate num_prefills; measure per-step host (CPU) time spent in get_prefill_chunk_plan across all layers vs a cached-once variant.

findings.claim: `get_prefill_chunk_plan` (vllm/v1/attention/backends/mla/sparse_swa.py) performs a Python `while` loop over `num_prefills` requests, calling `int(...item())` 2-4 times per inner iteration on CPU tensors (compressed_lens_cpu[i].item(), gather_lens_cpu[i].item()). This is O(num_prefills) per-element scalar extraction executed on the host. While these are CPU tensors (so no D2H sync), the pattern indexes element-by-element in Python; converting the two small tensors to Python lists once (`.tolist()`) before the loop avoids repeated per-element .item() overhead. Combined with the per-layer re-execution above, the host cost scales as num_layers * num_prefills.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark get_prefill_chunk_plan with num_prefills in {8,32,128}; compare .item()-in-loop vs .tolist()-then-loop.

In csrc/cpu/cpu_attn_impl.hpp, `calcu_kv_tile_pos` was refactored to take `(window_size, causal)` and now internally calls `calcu_sliding_window_size(window_size, causal)` on every invocation. This function is called inside the innermost per-q-tile loop of the attention main loop (lines ~1646 and ~1760) as well as twice per q-tile in the scheduler (lines ~457, ~499). Previously the left/right sliding-window sizes were computed ONCE and passed in as precomputed scalars. The new code recomputes the same branchy left/right window derivation for every KV-tile iteration. Worse, in AttentionMainLoop the code even precomputes `[sliding_window_left, sliding_window_right]` once per workitem_group (line ~1564) but then discards them and passes `sliding_window_size, current_group_causal` to calcu_kv_tile_pos, forcing redundant recomputation inside the hot loop. Recommend passing the precomputed left/right window sizes into calcu_kv_tile_pos instead of recomputing per tile.
findings.suggested_benchmark: CPU attention microbench (varlen_with_paged_kv) with long sequences / many KV tiles per q-tile to measure per-tile overhead of the inlined calcu_sliding_window_size branches

findings.claim: In the per-step dispatch path (GPUModelRunner around line 1129), the new block builds `req_ids = list(scheduler_output.num_scheduled_tokens.keys())` and calls `get_num_active_loras_for_dispatch(...)` on every execute_model call when LoRA is enabled. Inside that helper, `lora_state.get_activate_loras(req_ids)` iterates over all req_ids performing a dict.get and set insertion per request (O(num_reqs) Python-level work), and only its `len()` is used to compute num_active_loras. This is per-iteration overhead added to the hot decode path solely to produce a small integer for cudagraph key selection. The `list(...keys())` materialization plus set construction repeats every step; if only the count of distinct active LoRAs is needed, a counter maintained incrementally in LoraState (on add/remove_request) would avoid rebuilding the set each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-steady-state throughput/latency with LoRA enabled (many small requests) comparing per-step overhead of building the active-LoRA set each dispatch vs an incrementally maintained count.

In CPUAttentionMetadataBuilder.build() (called once per forward step — hot path), the new encoder-only branch allocates a fresh KV cache buffer every step via torch.zeros((total_block_num, num_kv_heads, block_size, 2*head_dim), dtype=self.dtype), stored as encoder_cache_tensor and passed through metadata to forward(). This is a per-step allocation whose size is driven by total_block_num = sum(ceil(seq_len/block_size)) — i.e. proportional to the full batch token count each step. Unlike the decoder path which reuses a persistent paged KV cache, this buffer is re-allocated (and zero-initialized) every iteration, adding malloc + memset O(total_tokens * num_kv_heads * 2*head_dim) work on the critical path. Consider reusing a preallocated/pooled buffer across steps and/or skipping the zero-init if the reshape_and_cache kernel plus kv_end_pos masking already covers all read positions.

release_device_memory_under_pressure() calls torch.accelerator.synchronize(device) followed by torch.accelerator.empty_cache() inside process_weights_after_loading's per-module loop. Both are expensive: synchronize() is a full device barrier that stalls the pipeline, and empty_cache() releases the caching allocator's reserved blocks back to the OS via cudaFree, which forces subsequent allocations to go through slow cudaMalloc again. Because this is invoked once per module (once for each module with a QuantizeMethodBase quant_method), on large quantized models with many layers this can be called hundreds of times during weight loading. On UMA/integrated GPUs where the guard actually fires, repeatedly synchronizing + tearing down and rebuilding the allocator cache substantially slows weight loading (the OS-thrash mitigation trades throughput for lower peak). The mitigation itself is intentional, but placing the call in a per-module loop rather than once after the loop amplifies the empty_cache/synchronize cost.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model weight-loading wall time for a large quantized model (many layers) on an integrated/UMA GPU with and without release_device_memory_under_pressure, and count how many times empty_cache/synchronize fire.

The Marconi admission optimization in `_mamba_block_aligned_split` can truncate `num_new_tokens` down to `num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` (rounded to block_size). While this is a caching-oriented optimization intended to increase future cross-request prefix reuse, it *reduces the number of tokens scheduled in the current step* for hybrid/mamba requests whenever an uncached common prefix >= block_size is detected. This deliberately chunks a prefill that could otherwise have been done in one step, forcing an extra scheduler iteration/forward for the remainder. On the throughput-sensitive prefill path this trades current-step throughput for speculative future cache hits; if the detected 'common prefix' is not actually reused by a later request (false positive from the longest_hit_length heuristic), it is pure overhead (extra prefill chunk, extra step) with no cache benefit — the same class as the historical hybrid prefix-caching throughput regressions.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Hybrid (mamba+full attn) prefill throughput with and without shared prefixes; measure tokens/step and TTFT when the heuristic fires but no later request reuses the prefix.

findings.claim: `num_uncached_common_prefix_tokens` is a coordinator-level mutable state set as a side-effect of find_longest_cache_hit and later read by the scheduler for a different request. This couples cache-hit lookup to scheduling via shared instance state rather than a return value. If find_longest_cache_hit is not called for a request (e.g. externally-cached/connector path or the value is stale from a prior request), the scheduler could apply an admission split based on a stale prefix length, causing an unnecessary prefill chunking (extra step) for a request that has no such common prefix. This staleness risk is a correctness-adjacent throughput hazard on the hybrid path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Interleave hybrid requests with and without shared prefixes and verify the admission split only fires for the intended request; measure spurious extra prefill steps.

store_mask() lost its constant-work template+multiply fast path. Parent code, when all groups are FullAttention or have block_size==lcm_block_size, returned `tuple([True]*n ...)` in O(num_chunks) with no per-block computation; and for the hybrid case it computed a per-segment template ONCE over a single lcm segment (find_longest_cache_hit with max_length=lcm_block_size) and replicated it with list-multiply (`template * n_segments`), i.e. O(chunks_per_segment) hit-solving + O(num_chunks) copy. The new code unconditionally loops over every group and calls manager_cls.reachable_block_mask(start_block=0, end_block=num_chunks, ...) over the FULL aligned length for each call. For long prefills (large aligned_token_len -> large num_chunks) and hybrid/SWA specs this is O(num_chunks) mask construction per group per store, executed in KVCacheStoreSendingThread._handle_request on every stored request. This is a hot send path; the loss of the single-segment template amortization can materially increase per-request store-side CPU on long sequences.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark store_mask(aligned_token_len) for a hybrid full+SWA config across increasing token_len (e.g. 4k/16k/64k), comparing parent template-multiply path vs new per-block reachable_block_mask loop; also end-to-end Mooncake store throughput on long-prefill workloads.

This revert undoes the warp-shuffle + vectorized-I/O optimization for silu_and_mul_per_block_quant_kernel, reintroducing a known performance regression. The parent (post-optimization) version used one warp per (token,group) with each lane owning EPT=group_size/32 contiguous elements read via a single coalesced wide vector load (InVec/OutVec reinterpret_cast), a pure warp-shuffle abs-max reduction (__shfl_xor_sync, no shared memory, no __syncthreads), and kWarpsPerBlock=4 groups packed into a 128-thread block. The revert restores the one-block-per-group design where each thread loads a SINGLE scalar element (token_input_gate[tid] / token_input_up[tid]) and writes a single scalar output (token_output[tid]), performs a shared-memory tree reduction with log2(group_size) __syncthreads barriers, and launches one group per block (dim3 block(group_size)). This matches the scalar-per-thread narrow-load and reduced-occupancy mechanism seen in the historical INT8-quant and RMS-norm/reduce-scatter cases; the original commit noted this design was memory-latency-bound and ~2x off peak.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark silu_and_mul_per_block_quant on typical MoE/FFN shapes (num_tokens large, hidden_size 4096-8192, group_size 128 and 64) for both fp8 and int8 outputs; compare kernel latency/throughput before vs after this revert.

findings.claim: In the fused path, `self.q_norm.weight.float() + 1.0` and `self.k_norm.weight.float() + 1.0` are recomputed on EVERY forward call (see `_project_qkv_gate` in qwen3_next.py). `.float()` allocates a new fp32 copy of the head_dim weight and `+ 1.0` launches another elementwise kernel — per attention layer, per forward step (including every decode token step). This is invariant work that only depends on module weights and should be precomputed once at __init__ (e.g. cache `self._q_norm_gamma = (self.q_norm.weight.float() + 1.0)` as a buffer) and reused. On the decode hot path (short sequences, many layers), these two tiny device allocations + kernel launches per layer add measurable CPU-launch/host overhead relative to the single fused kernel launch this PR is trying to save.

The new `_inflight_prefill_reserved_blocks()` (scheduler.py ~line 2140) sums `_request_remaining_blocks(req)` over all `self._inflight_prefills`, and it is invoked once per candidate async-KV-load request inside the waiting-scheduling loop. `_request_remaining_blocks` in turn calls `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate`, which iterates every single_type_manager to recompute the full-ISL block count from scratch each call. Since the reserved-blocks total is recomputed per candidate (and the inflight set is re-summed each time), admitting K async loads while N prefills are in flight is O(K*N*num_groups) per step. This is gated behind `if load_kv_async:` so it only fires on the KV-connector async-load path (cold-ish for standard non-P/D deployments), which limits blast radius, but under heavy disaggregated-prefill traffic with many in-flight prefills it is repeated hot-path work per schedule step.

With num_experts up to 256 this launches up to hundreds of tiny GPU kernels (indexed slice + fill) per MoE layer per forward, all serialized on the host — a per-element/per-expert scalar loop instead of a single vectorized op. This is the classic 'computation runs element-wise on host instead of vectorized' pattern and adds launch overhead on the hot MoE dispatch path.

The new hipBLASLt GEMM path runs one-time initialization checks on every linear-layer forward. `_rocm_aiter_hipb_mm_fp8_impl` (invoked via `hipb_mm_fp8` -> custom op -> from `AiterHipbMMPerTokenFp8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_scaled_mm`, i.e. every FP8 linear forward for both prefill and decode) calls `_ensure_hipb_mm_extension_initialized()`, which each call does `import aiter` and `torch.accelerator.current_device_index()` plus a `set` membership lookup. After the first init these are pure overhead repeated per matmul per layer per step. `torch.accelerator.current_device_index()` in particular is a host-side query on the hot path. This is init-shaped work (extension creation) placed on the per-iteration path; it should be hoisted to a one-time setup (e.g. `process_weights_after_loading`) so the hot forward path only does the GEMM.

In NixlEplbCommunicator, the parent implementation batched all experts received from a given peer into a single NIXL transfer: execute() built descriptors and called _create_peer_xfer(src, local_descs, remote_descs) + transfer(xfer_h) exactly ONCE per source rank (O(world_size) prep_xfer_dlist / get_xfer_descs / make_prepped_xfer / transfer calls). The new implementation moves descriptor construction and transfer issuance into add_recv(), which is called once per received expert in move_to_buffer's recv loop. Each add_recv now calls _create_peer_xfer (two get_xfer_descs + two prep_xfer_dlist + one make_prepped_xfer) and one transfer(), i.e. O(num_recv_experts) RDMA issuance instead of O(num_peers). For a layer where multiple experts are pulled from the same peer, this replaces one batched READ per peer with one READ per expert, multiplying per-transfer setup overhead (dlist prep, handle creation) and issuing many small RDMA reads instead of one coalesced read. This runs per MoE layer per rebalance step, so the cost scales with num_layers * num_recv_experts.
findings.suggested_benchmark: EPLB rebalance micro-benchmark measuring transfer_layer / move_to_buffer wall time with a topology where several experts are received from the same peer (e.g. world_size=8, many-to-one expert migration), comparing batched-per-peer vs per-expert NIXL issuance.

findings.claim: The new PowerPC/VSX FP16<->FP32 conversions in cpu_types_vsx.hpp implement the conversion via a scalar round-trip: they vec_xst the whole 16-lane register out to a stack buffer, run a scalar `for (int i=0;i<16;i++) c10::Half(temp_fp32[i])` / `float(temp_fp16[i])` loop, then reload with vec_xl. These conversions are invoked directly inside the hot all_reduce_sum_impl inner loop for c10::Half tensors (`vec_op::FP32Vec16 local_data_fp32(local_data)` on load and `vec_t reduced_data(local_data_fp32)` on store, per RANKS-1 remote operand each iteration). POWER9/10/11 VSX provides hardware half<->single conversion (xvcvhpsp / xvcvsphp), so a vectorized path exists but the code takes the scalar store/reload path instead. This makes fp16 SHM allreduce meaningfully slower than the analogous BF16Vec16 path (which uses vec_mergeh/vec_mergel vector ops) on the same platform.

findings.claim: In profile_cudagraph_memory the diff now performs a *full* encoder CUDA graph capture (encoder_cudagraph_manager.capture(graph_pool=encoder_profiling_pool)) purely for a memory estimate. Unlike the decoder path (which only profiles descs[:2], i.e. 2 samples, and extrapolates per-graph cost for the rest), the encoder path calls capture() which loops over ALL token_budgets and runs _capture_budget_graph for each. Each _capture_budget_graph enters `with torch.cuda.graph(graph, pool=...)`, and PyTorch's graph context manager internally runs gc.collect()/empty_cache() per capture. So every encoder budget graph is captured twice at startup: once during profiling and again during the real capture in capture_model. This doubles encoder capture time and repeats the expensive per-capture gc/empty_cache — the exact repeated-per-capture wasted-work mechanism from the historical case.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure model startup/warmup time for a multimodal model with cudagraph_mm_encoder enabled and several encoder_cudagraph_token_budgets; compare against parent to isolate the added profiling-time full encoder capture.

findings.claim: The temporary profiling manager and the persistent manager are created independently: profile_cudagraph_memory builds a throwaway EncoderCudaGraphManager via _create_encoder_cudagraph_manager(), captures all budget graphs, then clear()s them; capture_model later creates the persistent manager and captures the same budgets again. The results of the profiling capture are discarded (clear() drops budget_graphs and graph_pool), so all encoder graph capture work done during profiling is thrown away with no reuse. Consider profiling only 1-2 encoder budgets and extrapolating, mirroring the decoder descs[:2] approach, to avoid full recapture at profiling time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument capture time separately for profiling-phase encoder capture vs real encoder capture to confirm duplicated work.

findings.claim: In the sparse MLA decode hot path build(), the new metadata fingerprinting reads `common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu[:num_reqs].numpy()` to compute `clamped_seq_lens` for `metadata_key`. `seq_lens_cpu` is a lazily-materialized property (backend.py:442-445) that, when `_seq_lens_cpu is None`, executes `self.seq_lens.to('cpu')` — a blocking device->host copy that forces a full CUDA/HIP sync every decode step. The property is explicitly `@deprecated` with the note 'Prefer using device seq_lens directly to avoid implicit H<>D sync which breaks full async scheduling.' The parent code never touched seq_lens_cpu in this builder (it used only query_start_loc_cpu, which is already on host). So the fingerprint added to skip get_mla_metadata_v1 launches introduces a per-step blocking D2H sync that can cost more than the launch it saves, and can serialize the decode step / break async scheduling. On MI355X TP4 this is squarely on the decode-step critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DSv3.2 MI355X TP4 decode-only latency: measure per-step build() time and end-to-end decode TPOT with and without the seq_lens_cpu fingerprint; profile for hipMemcpyDtoH / stream sync in the decode step.

findings.claim: The metadata_key fingerprint uses `clamped_seq_lens.tobytes()`. Even setting aside the D2H sync, hashing/comparing the full per-request clamped seq-len byte string every decode step is O(num_reqs) host work. During steady decode the seq_lens change every step (each request advances by 1 token) until they hit the topk_tokens clamp, so the fingerprint will frequently miss and the get_mla_metadata_v1 launch will still run — the intended cache-skip benefit only materializes once most requests are clamped, while the D2H + tobytes cost is paid unconditionally on every step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Instrument cache hit-rate of _prev_metadata_key over a decode run; compare launch-skip savings vs per-step fingerprint overhead.

In `_get_numactl_worker_args`, when the user did not pass `--numa-bind-cpus`, the worker now auto-binds to PCT priority cores via `_maybe_get_pct_cpu_binding([numa_node])`. On a matching Granite Rapids SKU (e.g. 6776P), this narrows each worker's cpuset from the full NUMA node (~32 threads) to only the priority-core subset (`cpu_id % stride in (0,1)` → 8 threads on the tested node). This is the *intended* placement change, but it is a significant, silently-triggered CPU-affinity change (from `--cpunodebind=N` covering all node cores to `--physcpubind=<8 cores>`) that fires automatically on any host whose `/proc/cpuinfo` SKU string and cpu0 `highest_perf` happen to match the hard-coded table. If PCT is not actually engaged/enabled on such a host, workers get pinned to a 4x-smaller core set, throttling per-worker CPU-bound throughput (tokenization, sampling, host-side overheads). Recommend an explicit opt-in/opt-out flag rather than purely empirical auto-detection, and a benchmark comparing per-node vs PCT-subset binding on a matching SKU with PCT disabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On a Xeon 6776P (or SKU spoofed to match the table) with PCT disabled, measure end-to-end throughput with worker binding = full NUMA node vs PCT priority-core subset; verify no regression on CPU-bound stages.

This PR removes the inplace fused-experts mechanism entirely. At parent, the MoE output could reuse the input `hidden_states` buffer (out_hidden_states = hidden_states if inplace) rather than allocating a fresh tensor. After this change, fused_experts_impl and FusedMoEKernelModularImpl unconditionally do `out_hidden_states = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)` / `output = torch.empty_like(hidden_states)`, adding one extra activation-sized allocation per MoE layer per forward on paths that previously ran inplace (e.g. unquantized/int8/wna16/fp8/mxfp4 modular kernels via the oracle `inplace=` args, and the marlin/wna16/moe_wna16/bitsandbytes `fused_experts(..., inplace=...)` callers). This is a real hot-path (per-decode-step, per-MoE-layer) code form matching the diff.

The fused Lamport kernel now dispatches at runtime instead of via a compile pass that pre-allocated the workspace during setup. If get_allreduce_workspace is not warmed up before the hot path, the first decode step that hits the fused path will allocate the IPC Lamport workspace (cudaMalloc + all_gather_object IPC handle exchange) inline. get_allreduce_workspace is cached, but the very first hot-path call incurs a cross-rank all_gather_object collective and cudaMalloc. Ensure the runtime path pre-allocates the workspace at model init (as the removed MiniMaxQKNormPass.__init__ did) rather than lazily on first forward.

findings.claim: In mhc_post_tilelang the main loop over `T.ceildiv(h, h_blk)` is changed from `T.Pipelined(..., num_stages=2)` to `T.Serial(...)`. The 2-stage software pipeline previously overlapped the global-memory loads of `b`/`d` (T.copy(b[i_n,0,...], b_shared) / T.copy(d[...], d_shared)) with the compute of the prior iteration, hiding DRAM/async-copy latency. With T.Serial the load->compute->store becomes strictly sequential per h-block, exposing global-memory load latency in the kernel's hot loop and reducing achievable occupancy/overlap. This restructures the parallelization/overlap scheme rather than merely tuning launch params, matching the kernel-occupancy-redesign mechanism (but in the regressing direction — overlap removed).

When vllm_is_batch_invariant() is true, it forces a single fixed large-M tiling

The batch-invariant path (VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT) forces the large-M `sm100_fp4_config_default`/`sm120_fp4_config_default` tiling (e.g. TileShape Shape<_256,_256,_256>, PerSmTileShape_MNK Shape<_128,_256,_256>) for ALL M, bypassing the M-based dispatch that would otherwise select `config_M16`/`config_M256` for small batches. For small-M (decode, M=1..256) GEMMs this uses an oversized tile with poor SM occupancy and wasted compute vs the tuned small-M configs. This matches the kernel-config-tuning / large-tile-for-small-M mechanism from the priors. It is, however, gated strictly behind the opt-in VLLM_BATCH_INVARIANT deterministic mode, where a fixed large-M tiling + PersistentScheduler is a documented numerical-invariance requirement (static_assert forbidding stream-K/split-K), so the slow path is a correctness fallback, not the default hot path. Impact is confined to users who explicitly enable deterministic execution.

chunk_kda_fwd adds a new eager elementwise op `g = g * RCP_LN2` on the cumulative-gate tensor (shape ~[B,T,H,K]) right after chunk_local_cumsum, on the chunked prefill path. This launches an extra full-tensor CUDA multiply kernel per forward call. It is a small O(B*T*H*K) pass and not a host-scalar loop, but it is redundant work that could have been folded into the preceding chunk_local_cumsum (scale g there) or into the consuming kernels via a scalar multiply on the exponent. As written it is an added standalone kernel launch + memory read/write of the whole g tensor on every chunk prefill.

此改动触及 activation-recompute（memory-footprint），该类问题历史上主要在 [MoE (shared experts, TE grouped MLP), optionally MLA, context parallelism (CP>1) for CP path; recompute_granularity=full, recompute_method=uniform, recompute_num_layers=1 for memory path; also relevant to MoE/EP and TP, MoE / expert parallelism (GroupedMLP, TEGroupedMLP); also applies to MLA (mla_up_proj) and layernorms] 配置下显现；This revert removes an Inductor materialization-heuristic patch (should_realize_on_reuse). The patch's purpose was to prevent Inductor from inlining/recomputing

This commit reverts PR #42686, removing `_patch_should_realize_on_reuse()` from vllm/env_override.py. Per the removed code's own docstring, that patch overrode Inductor's `StorageBox.should_realize_on_reuse` materialization heuristic on torch 2.11.0 specifically to prevent inlining/recomputing chains of reused tensors (e.g., the residual in fused_add_rms_norm) during whole-model fullgraph compilation. With the patch removed, on torch==2.11.0 Inductor reverts to the un-patched heuristic and will inline+recompute these reused sub-expressions, which then cascades through the model and adds redundant compute to every forward pass (runtime latency/throughput regression). The mechanism matches the 'recompute every iteration' symptom, though the cause is a removed compile-time lowering heuristic rather than vLLM Python hot-path code. Mitigating context: (1) this is a revert of a recently added patch, restoring prior baseline behavior and presumably fixing fullgraph-compilation breakage that the patch introduced; (2) the effect is strictly gated to torch==2.11.0 (fixed upstream in torch 2.12 via pytorch/pytorch#176994). So the regression, if any, is narrow-version-scoped and may be an intentional correctness-over-perf tradeoff.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On torch==2.11.0, run an end-to-end latency/throughput benchmark (e.g. benchmarks/benchmark_latency.py) with full torch.compile fullgraph mode on a residual-heavy model (Llama-class using fused_add_rms_norm), comparing this commit vs its parent to measure per-forward recomputation overhead.

In mhc.py, MHCPreOp.forward_hip and MHCPostOp.forward_hip previously dispatched to the AITER fused kernels (torch.ops.vllm.mhc_pre_aiter / mhc_post_aiter) when hidden_size % 256 == 0, falling back to the pure-PyTorch mhc_kernels.mhc_pre_torch / mhc_post_torch otherwise. This diff comments out the entire AITER branch, so ALL ROCm calls now unconditionally take the slower PyTorch reference path (mhc_pre_torch / mhc_post_torch). For DeepSeek V4 hidden sizes divisible by 256 (the common case), this removes the specialized fused backend on the per-decoder-layer hot path, a throughput/latency regression. This is the inverse of the historical fused-backend-swap pattern (removing rather than adding the AITER path). It is a deliberate accuracy bugfix (aiter kernel has accuracy issues at large token counts), but it does trade off performance until aiter is upgraded.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek V4 decode/prefill throughput on ROCm (MI3xx) with hidden_size % 256 == 0, comparing mhc_pre/mhc_post PyTorch reference vs AITER fused kernel.

findings.claim: Same AITER-path removal applies to MHCPostOp.forward_hip (mhc_post_aiter -> mhc_post_torch). Both hc_pre and hc_post run once per DeepseekV4DecoderLayer per forward, so the reference-path fallback compounds across all layers.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Per-layer microbenchmark of mhc_post_torch vs mhc_post_aiter for hidden_size=7168 on ROCm.

findings.claim: In _build_fp8_prefill_ps_metadata (called from build() every step whenever prefill tokens are present and FP8 MLA prefill is enabled), `num_partial_tiles = int(self.fp8_ps_reduce_indptr[-1].item())` reads the last element of a device tensor (reduce_indptr lives on `device`). This forces a blocking device->host sync on the metadata-build path on every prefill step. The code comment explicitly acknowledges this ('build is allowed to incur an occasional sync'), but chunked-prefill batches recur frequently, so the sync is per-build, not occasional. This matches the historical blocking-d2h-sync pattern where a device value is pulled to host during metadata build. Ideally num_partial_tiles could be derived from a CPU-side reduce_indptr or an upper bound to avoid the stall.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Chunked-prefill throughput/latency on gfx950 with FP8 MLA enabled; profile build() for a hipStreamSynchronize induced by .item() on reduce_indptr.

Two nested `def check():` closures are (re)defined on every iteration of the `while True` polling loops in the writer (acquire-write) and reader (acquire-read) hot paths. These closures capture `metadata_buffer`/`self` and are freshly created each loop turn; when spinloop is passed `check` as a Python callback it invokes `PyObject_CallNoArgs` in a tight C loop, and the pre-check `if ... and not check()` plus the trailing `if not check()` add repeated Python-level function-object creation and calls per spin iteration. On the ring-buffer fast path (frequent reads) this adds Python call overhead compared to the original inline `sum(metadata_buffer[1:])` / flag comparisons that avoided constructing a closure each turn.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark enqueue/dequeue loop throughput with VLLM_USE_SPINLOOP_EXT=0 vs baseline to isolate the added closure-definition + double-check() overhead on the hot busy-poll path.

The new heartbeat tracking dicts on the D-side scheduler (`_heartbeat_by_engine: dict[EngineId, HeartbeatInfo]` and `_heartbeat_req_engine: dict[ReqId, tuple]`) grow when `on_new_request` is called for every do_remote_prefill request, but are only pruned via `_stop_heartbeat`, which is invoked from `update_connector_output` (on finished_recving) and `request_finished`. If a D-side request's KV recv never completes AND request_finished is never invoked for that req_id (e.g. certain abort/error paths, or requests that are dropped before reaching finished_recving), the entry leaks. Unlike the prior state that also had abort-timeout pruning on the worker, these scheduler-side dicts have no timeout-based sweep — they rely entirely on the two lifecycle hooks firing. This is the same 'unpruned state only cleaned on notif/finish' mechanism as the historical NIXL _reqs_to_process leak.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run PD disaggregation with a stream of do_remote_prefill requests where a fraction are aborted before finished_recving; monitor len(_heartbeat_by_engine[*].req_ids) and _heartbeat_req_engine over time for monotonic growth.

In CutlassFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.apply_scaled_mm (a per-forward hot path — called from apply_weights on every linear invocation), when the weight dims (K,N) are not 16-aligned, the *static* weight B is re-padded on every single forward: `B = torch.nn.functional.pad(B.t().contiguous(), (0, pad_k, 0, pad_n)).t()`. This does (1) a transpose, (2) a full `.contiguous()` materialization/copy of the weight, (3) an F.pad allocation+copy — all redundant since B (and pad_k/pad_n, being derived from the static weight shape) never change between calls. Similarly `bias` and per-channel `Bs` are re-padded each forward. For models like Qwen2.5-VL vision MLP (the stated target, e.g. N=3420/6840), every forward pays this weight-copy tax. The padded weight/bias/scale should be computed once in process_weights_after_loading (which for CutlassFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel is currently a no-op inherited from FP8ScaledMMLinearKernel) instead of on the hot path. This matches the historical 'wasteful layout/copy kernels' pattern (cf. the SM100 blockwise FP8 pad-.contiguous()-then-slice case).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark CutlassFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel forward throughput/latency for an unaligned-N/K weight (e.g. Qwen2.5-VL vision MLP dims N=3420,K=1280) over many iterations; compare against pre-padded weight in process_weights_after_loading to isolate the per-call B.t().contiguous()+pad cost.

findings.claim: The output slice+contiguous `output = output[..., :N].contiguous()` when pad_n>0 forces an extra full copy of the output tensor on every forward to drop the padded N columns. Since pad_n is at most 15 columns of padding, the entire MxN_padded output is re-materialized each call just to trim a fixed padding. Combined with the padded GEMM writing extra columns, this is wasted per-call copy work on the hot path proportional to output size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure latency delta of the pad_n>0 branch with vs without the trailing output[..., :N].contiguous() for large M (e.g. prefill batch) to quantify the extra output copy.

The diff removes the dedicated `async_output_thread` (ThreadPoolExecutor with max_workers=1) that was created only when `max_concurrent_batches > 1` (i.e. async scheduling enabled). Previously, for an AsyncModelRunnerOutput the executor submitted `result.get_output()` to that background thread and returned the thread's Future, so the blocking GPU->CPU sync (`AsyncOutput.get_output()` calls `self.copy_event.synchronize()` in vllm/v1/worker/gpu/async_utils.py:48) ran off the main scheduling thread. This allowed the engine to overlap the previous batch's output copy/sync with scheduling/submission of the next batch — the whole point of async scheduling. After this change, `collective_rpc` returns an `AsyncOutputFuture` whose `get_output()` is only invoked lazily inside `.result()`, executed on whatever (main engine) thread later calls `.result()`. If `.result()` is called on the critical scheduling thread before the next batch is dispatched, `copy_event.synchronize()` now blocks that thread, serializing GPU-copy with scheduling and defeating async-scheduling overlap. This resembles a config/path-toggle where a feature (background offload) that used to be active under `async_scheduling` is no longer taken.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Enable async scheduling (scheduler_config.async_scheduling=True) on a single-GPU UniProcExecutor and measure decode throughput / inter-token latency vs parent commit; verify next-batch submission overlaps with prior batch output copy (copy_event.synchronize).
