The diff flips the default of VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND from "0" to "1" in vllm/envs.py (`int(os.getenv("VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND", "0"))` -> "1"). Per the comment in envs.py and the branch in vllm/v1/executor/abstract.py:Executor.get_class (`if envs.VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND: executor_class = RayExecutorV2 else: RayDistributedExecutor`), this static default change reroutes ALL default Ray deployments (distributed_executor_backend="ray") from the compiled-graph (Ray Compiled DAG / aDAG) backend to the MQ (message-queue) based RayExecutorV2. The compiled-graph backend was specifically designed to shrink per-step control-plane latency (NCCL/SHM channels, avoiding per-call Ray RPC dispatch); switching the default to the MQ path affects the hot per-step control path used by execute_model/sample_tokens/collective_rpc. This is a config-default flip choosing a different (potentially slower on the hot dispatch path) execution path by default, matching the historical config-toggle-perf-feature mechanism. The TYPE_CHECKING annotation default (VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND: bool = False at line 63) was NOT updated to match, which is inconsistent but not itself a perf bug.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Multi-node/multi-GPU TP+PP distributed serving throughput and per-step latency with distributed_executor_backend="ray", comparing VLLM_USE_RAY_V2_EXECUTOR_BACKEND=0 (compiled-graph RayDistributedExecutor) vs =1 (MQ-based RayExecutorV2): measure inter-token latency, decode step time, and control-plane dispatch overhead.

findings.claim: The diff flips the static default `enable_flashinfer_autotune` from True to False in OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_01/02/03 (O2 is the default optimization level). At kernel_warmup time (vllm/model_executor/warmup/kernel_warmup.py:39-46), when this is False the code logs 'Skipping FlashInfer autotune because it is disabled.' and skips the flashinfer_autotune() warmup call. Per flashinfer_autotune()'s own docstring, without autotuning FlashInfer falls back to heuristic kernel selection which 'may be significantly slower'. On Hopper (SM 9.0) / Blackwell (SM 10.0) GPUs with FlashInfer installed, this is a runtime throughput/latency regression on the default and higher optimization levels. The change is an intentional, temporary correctness workaround (comment references flashinfer-ai/flashinfer#3197), i.e. deliberately trading performance for correctness — matching the correctness-forces-slow-path pattern. Should be reverted once the upstream FlashInfer correctness bug is fixed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/latency benchmark on Hopper/Blackwell with a FlashInfer attention backend at default -O2, comparing autotune enabled vs disabled (e.g. vllm bench serve / benchmark_throughput on an FP8 or nvfp4 model).

findings.claim: In the new forward_xpu hot sampling path (runs once per decode step), the RNG seed is extracted every call via `state = generator.get_state(); seed, offset = state.view(torch.int64); seeds = torch.tensor([seed, offset], ..., device='cpu')`. Unpacking `state.view(torch.int64)` into Python scalars indexes/reads the generator state, and re-materializing them into a new CPU tensor from a Python list every step is per-iteration host work that can force a host/device round-trip on the sampling critical path. This matches the historical blocking-d2h-sync / list-tensor-construction pattern; prefer slicing/reusing the state tensor directly rather than element-wise unpacking + list reconstruction.
findings.suggested_benchmark: XPU decode sampling latency with top-k/top-p enabled; compare per-step overhead of seed extraction vs a cached/sliced state tensor

findings.claim: `k = k.to(torch.int64)` in forward_xpu allocates a fresh int64 device tensor and does an int32->int64 conversion on every sampling step (hot decode path). Since top_k is stored as int32 in the input batch and doesn't change, this per-step device allocation/copy is redundant; casting once upstream or letting the kernel accept int32 would avoid the recurring allocation. It is small (batch-sized) so impact is minor.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark forward_xpu per-call cost with vs without per-step k.to(int64) at various batch sizes

findings.claim: The default of VLLM_MULTI_STREAM_GEMM_TOKEN_THRESHOLD is lowered from 4096 to 1024 in vllm/envs.py (both the TYPE_CHECKING hint and the getenv lambda). This threshold gates the DSv4 multi-stream pre-attention GEMM overlap path in deepseek_v4_attention.py: `execute_in_parallel(..., enable=hidden_states.shape[0] <= envs.VLLM_MULTI_STREAM_GEMM_TOKEN_THRESHOLD)`. The multi-stream path (aux-stream GEMMs overlapping the FP8 main GEMM, documented as a 5-45% win when the FP8 GEMM has idle SMs) is enabled only when token count <= threshold. Lowering 4096->1024 means batches with 1024 < num_tokens <= 4096 now take the serial (non-overlapped) path instead of the previously-enabled multi-stream path. If the true empirical crossover on the target GPUs is above 1024, this is a latency regression for that batch-size band, matching the historical 'static default value chooses slow path' pattern. This is a hot path (per-layer, per-forward on DeepSeek V4).
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek-V4 prefill/decode latency sweep over batch token counts in {512, 1024, 2048, 3072, 4096} on B200/B300, comparing VLLM_MULTI_STREAM_GEMM_TOKEN_THRESHOLD=1024 vs 4096 to confirm the crossover point and that no regression exists in the 1024-4096 band.

In `maybe_make_thread_pool`, the `TokenizerPool` wrapper routes every public tokenizer call (`__call__`, `encode`, `decode`, `batch_decode`, `apply_chat_template`, etc.) through `_borrow_from_pool()`. When the bounded pool is exhausted (queue.Empty), it falls back to `copy.deepcopy(og_tokenizer)` — a full deep copy of the Rust fast-tokenizer backend — on the per-request tokenization/detokenization hot path. The pool holds only `renderer_num_workers + 1` copies (default = 2). Under concurrent load with more in-flight tokenizer calls than pool copies (e.g. AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer plus MM processor plus chat-template rendering concurrently), each excess call incurs a synchronous deepcopy of the whole tokenizer, which then also grows the pool unbounded (finally: `tokenizer_pool.put(tok)` always re-inserts, so transient bursts permanently inflate the pool and its resident memory). This adds substantial constant per-call cost on hot detokenize/encode paths compared to the previous plain method calls.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Concurrent chat/completions load test (e.g. 100+ concurrent streaming requests) measuring per-request tokenize/detokenize latency and RSS growth, comparing pool sizing renderer_num_workers=1 (copies=2) vs high concurrency.

findings.claim: In the hot path rocm_unquantized_gemm_impl (per-linear-layer call for every unquantized GEMM on ROCm), a new AITER tuned-GEMM backend is swapped in: when use_skinny is False and rocm_aiter_ops.is_tgemm_enabled() is True, it does `from aiter.tuned_gemm import tgemm; return tgemm.mm(x, weight, bias)` instead of torch.nn.functional.linear. This is exactly the 'swap to an existing specialized/fused backend' pattern. Two risks: (1) the `from aiter.tuned_gemm import tgemm` import runs on every call on the hot path — although Python caches modules in sys.modules, the import statement + attribute lookup still adds per-call overhead vs. hoisting it to module scope; (2) tgemm.mm dispatches to AITER-tuned kernels that are only guaranteed beneficial on the gated shapes/arch (gfx950). If the tuned GEMM is slower than hipBLASLt/torch linear for the actual attention-projection shapes at runtime, this becomes a throughput regression on the default-enabled path (VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_LINEAR defaults to True).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark rocm_unquantized_gemm_impl on gfx950 comparing tgemm.mm vs torch.nn.functional.linear across attention QKV/O-projection shapes and batch sizes; measure end-to-end decode/prefill throughput with VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_LINEAR on vs off.

findings.claim: This diff is a correctness fix that RESTORES (does not introduce) the intended per-layer cudagraph-capture optimization. The parent code patched `gc.collect`/`torch.accelerator.empty_cache` with zero-arg `lambda: None` no-ops during piecewise cudagraph capture. This matches the historical 'cudagraph-enablement' pattern: no-op'ing empty_cache/gc during capture because 'running gc again and again across layers will make the cudagraph capture very slow.' However, `gc.collect` is legitimately called with a generation argument (e.g. `gc.collect(0/1/2)` in vllm/utils/gc_utils.py:104-106) and empty_cache/collect may be invoked with args by underlying code; a zero-arg lambda would raise TypeError, breaking the no-op patch and either aborting capture or letting the expensive gc/empty_cache calls re-run per layer. Changing to `lambda *args, **kwargs: None` correctly absorbs any call signature, ensuring the no-op suppression actually holds across all layers. No new regression is introduced; the change is neutral-to-positive on compile/capture time.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure piecewise cudagraph capture wall-time (startup/compile phase) with gc_disable=True to confirm gc.collect/empty_cache remain suppressed across all captured subgraphs and capture time is not increased.

findings.claim: The new code inserts `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` inside `_ensure_workspace_size`, directly on the workspace resize/grow path. `empty_cache()` triggers a full `cudaFree` of all unused cached segments and forces a device synchronization (it drains outstanding work and blocks). This is a notoriously expensive, synchronizing operation. Although workspace growth is normally guarded by `_locked` (locked after warmup), the `get_simultaneous` -> `_ensure_workspace_size` path is called from real attention/MoE forward code (flash_attn.py, flashmla_sparse.py, modular_kernel.py, sparse_attn_indexer.py). Any time the workspace legitimately grows (e.g. an unlocked path, elastic-EP rescale, or a larger request than seen during warmup), each grow now pays a synchronous empty_cache stall instead of just letting the caching allocator reuse a cached segment. This converts a cheap allocation into a global allocator flush + sync.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench WorkspaceManager._ensure_workspace_size on the unlocked/grow path (repeated growing allocations) measuring wall time with vs without torch.accelerator.empty_cache(); also measure a forward pass that triggers workspace regrowth.

findings.claim: The new PyAV `decode_frames` does a per-frame `container.seek(pts, stream=stream)` + `next(container.decode(video=0))` loop that decodes exactly ONE frame per seek. For inter (non-key) frames, `seek()` lands on the preceding keyframe, so each sampled frame forces the codec to re-establish decode state from a keyframe. When sampling a moderate/dense set of frames (or when `num_frames == total_frames_num`, i.e. num_frames=-1, where `compute_frames_index_to_sample` returns `list(range(total_frames))`), this per-frame seek pattern re-decodes overlapping GOP prefixes repeatedly and is far more expensive than the OpenCV backend's sequential single-pass grab/retrieve over the stream. This is the inverse of the historical opencv_dynamic optimization (compute indices up front then cheaply grab), reintroduced as a seek-per-frame scan whose cost scales with (num_sampled_frames * GOP_size) rather than the video length as the docstring claims.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark VideoBackend.load_bytes with backend='pyav' vs backend='opencv' on a long high-fps H.264 clip with large GOP, for num_frames=-1 (all frames) and dense uniform sampling (e.g. num_frames=256); compare wall time and CPU decode work.

findings.claim: For the all-frames / dense-sampling case, PyAV's per-frame seek loop calls `next(container.decode(video=0), None)` — creating a fresh decode generator every iteration after a seek. When consecutive requested indices fall within the same GOP, the OpenCV path reads them sequentially in one pass, whereas the PyAV path seeks back to the keyframe and re-decodes the GOP prefix for each index. This makes the pyav codec asymptotically slower for dense sampling despite being the intended default per the docstring ('Defaults to "pyav"'), so throughput of video-heavy serving workloads that request many frames could regress relative to the sequential decode.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure decode time for pyav backend as a function of GOP size and number of requested frames within a single GOP; verify O(frames*GOP) blow-up vs opencv sequential O(max_frame_idx).

findings.claim: This diff is a FIX (not a regression) for the historical quadratic-python-structure pattern. In StructuredOutputManager.should_advance (v1/structured_output/__init__.py:~331), the reasoner's streaming check runs per structured-output request on every decode step while reasoning is ongoing. Before this commit, GptOssReasoningParser had no is_reasoning_end_streaming override, so it fell through to the base ReasoningParser.is_reasoning_end_streaming default (abs_reasoning_parsers.py:~93) which calls self.is_reasoning_end(input_ids) over the ENTIRE all_token_ids. GptOss's is_reasoning_end scans backwards across the whole current message (up to an <|end|> boundary), so per-step cost grew with sequence length -> O(n^2) per sequence. The added override bounds the scan to a fixed window (reasoning_end_token_ids_prefix + reasoning_max_num_between_tokens + reasoning_end_token_ids_suffix + len(delta_ids)) via is_reasoning_end(input_ids[n-window:]), making per-step cost constant for normal decode (delta==1). No new quadratic hot path is introduced.

此改动触及 redundant-load-startup-work（io-startup），该类问题历史上主要在 [Megatron-FSDP with nccl_ub (NCCL user buffers) + fsdp_double_buffer required, large-scale / high data-parallel (many ranks); many dataset file prefixes (hundreds to thousands), virtual pipeline parallelism (VPP) with pipeline parallelism] 配置下显现；This diff is a pure startup-latency optimization: it parallelizes torch/transformers imports on a background thread, prewarms the forkserver process to avoid sp

The new `_startup_prefetch_weights` in api_server.py performs a full parent-side read of ALL weight shards into the OS page cache (open + read every byte, 8-thread pool, 16MB blocks) from the APIServer process, duplicating the exact same read work that the existing in-child `_prefetch_all_checkpoints`/`_prefetch_checkpoint` (weight_utils.py) already does. On single-node single-instance setups (the common case, where `client_config['input_address']` is absent) this is not merely a page-cache warm-up: (1) if the engine child forks/starts before the parent read completes, both processes contend for the same disk bandwidth and I/O queues, potentially slowing the engine's own read; (2) the parent thread reads bytes it immediately discards (`while f.read(block_size): pass`), so on a cold NVMe/network filesystem the head-start benefit can be offset by read contention. The guard only excludes headless API-only workers, not TP/PP-sharded engines where the in-child prefetch reads a rank-sliced subset (`sorted_files[rank::world_size]`) while the parent unconditionally reads the whole set — so the parent reads shards no local rank needs, wasting bandwidth.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-cache time-to-first-load for a large (tens-of-GB) local safetensors model with a single engine vs. with parent+child concurrent prefetch, on both fast-NVMe and network/HDD storage; also TP=4 where in-child prefetch is rank-sliced.

findings.claim: A new `@torch.compile(dynamic=True)` decorator is applied to `_bicubic_resize_and_normalize` (nano_nemotron_vl.py ~line 65), which is invoked on the CPU-side multimodal image/video preprocessing path (from dynamic_preprocess, apply_params, and video_to_pixel_values). torch.compile guards on input shapes; even with dynamic=True, the very first calls trigger a Dynamo trace + Inductor codegen (compilation), which is expensive one-time (potentially many seconds) work placed on the per-request preprocessing path. Worse, image/video preprocessing shapes here are highly variable (aspect-preserving target_h/target_w, dtype variants, size=None vs tuple, norm_mean/std None vs tensor). Each new combination of arg presence (size None vs not; norm None vs not) forces a separate recompile / guard specialization. Since these vary per image, this can cause repeated recompilation storms, adding large latency to preprocessing rather than accelerating it. bicubic interpolate + normalize is memory-bound elementwise/interp work that gains little from Inductor fusion, so the compile cost likely outweighs any kernel speedup.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end multimodal preprocessing latency (image + video) with a variety of image resolutions/aspect ratios on first-call (cold compile) and steady state; compare against parent using eager _bicubic_from_ndarray. The new Gemma4 routing Triton kernel is launched with a hardcoded num_warps=1 and a grid of (T,) — one program (single warp = 32 threads) per token. Each program does a full tl.sort over BLOCK_E=next_power_of_2(E)=128 elements plus load/exp/store. With num_warps=1 the kernel uses only 32 threads per token-row, which severely under-utilizes the SM for the sort/reduction work and can leave the GPU compute-bound and occupancy-limited. For long sequences (test even parametrizes num_tokens=250000) this launches 250k tiny single-warp programs. This is the classic suboptimal-kernel-gate/config shape: a fast fused kernel exists but its launch configuration constrains it to a slow regime. Recommend autotuning num_warps (e.g. 2/4/8) as a function of BLOCK_E, or exposing it rather than fixing num_warps=1.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench gemma4_fused_routing_kernel_triton vs gemma4_routing_function_torch on E=128, topk=8 across T in {1,128,2048,32768,250000} and dtypes {bf16,fp16,fp32}, sweeping num_warps in {1,2,4,8}; measure kernel latency + end-to-end MoE forward.

findings.claim: NixlEplbCommunicator._get_or_create_xfer caches NIXL handles in self._xfer_cache keyed on (src, total_bytes, recv_offset). recv_offset is a *running* offset packed sequentially over only the peers that actually participate in a given rebalance round (recv_offset += actual_total_bytes for each active src). Across rebalance rounds the set of active peers and their byte counts change, so the same logical src<->dst transfer maps to many different (total_bytes, recv_offset) keys over the process lifetime. Each new key allocates a local dlist, remote dlist and xfer handle via prep_xfer_dlist/make_prepped_xfer that are only ever released in __del__ or on an exception, never on successful execute(). Because EPLB rebalance runs repeatedly (every step_interval, potentially thousands of times over a long-running server), the cache and its underlying NIXL/UCX registered descriptors grow monotonically -- an unbounded native-memory leak rather than a bounded per-cycle peak.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Run repeated EPLB rebalances (many step_interval cycles) with backend='nixl' under a shifting expert-load pattern so recv_offset/total_bytes vary; monitor len(communicator._xfer_cache) and NIXL/UCX registered-memory/RSS over time for monotonic growth.

findings.claim: The new guard `_is_all_reqs_chunked_prefill()` must be evaluated identically (same value) on BOTH the sender (last PP rank) and every receiver rank; otherwise a collective-participation mismatch occurs. If any rank's `discard_request_mask.np[:num_reqs].all()` diverges (e.g. num_reqs=0 makes `all()` return True on an empty slice, or masks not yet synced across ranks), the broadcast is skipped on one side but issued on the other, causing an NCCL hang -> pipeline stall (the exact 'stuck chunked pipeline' symptom class). The guard predicate must be provably rank-consistent; verify the empty-batch case where `.all()` on an empty array yields True.

findings.claim: In megatron_rl_inference_mode, the inference-side cuda_graph_scope is changed from a hardcoded `[]` (empty list = full-layer / whole-layer capture during decode) to `args.cuda_graph_scope`. If the user has not passed `--cuda-graph-scope=full_iteration_inference` (or leaves it empty/at a training-oriented value), the inference decode path will now capture a different (possibly less complete, or no) CUDA-graph scope than before. Per dynamic_engine.py:343-347, when `CudaGraphScope.full_iteration_inference not in cuda_graph_scope`, no inference CUDA graphs are created ('This will not create inference CUDA graphs. Use --cuda-graph-scope=full_iteration_inference instead'). Thus users who previously benefited from the implicit full-layer inference capture (via the `[]` sentinel) can silently lose CUDA-graph acceleration during the decode-heavy rollout phase, increasing per-step kernel-launch overhead / GPU bubbles. This directly matches the historical cudagraph-enablement pattern where the full-iteration inference graph capture is gated on `cuda_graph_scope` membership.
findings.suggested_benchmark: RL rollout decode throughput (tokens/s and toks/s/gpu) with and without --cuda-graph-scope=full_iteration_inference, comparing to the prior implicit `[]` full-layer capture behavior.

findings.claim: In FlashAttentionImpl.forward (flash_attn.py, prefill branch), `_make_mm_prefix_mask_mod(max_ranges)` is called on every forward invocation whenever mm_prefix_range_tensor is present and FA4 is active. This function imports cutlass/cutlass.cute and defines+JIT-decorates a `@cute.jit` closure each call. Constructing a fresh mask_mod closure (and its CuTE-DSL compilation) per-step on the hot attention path is expensive and is not cached/memoized. Because `max_ranges` is the only argument (a small integer), the JIT-compiled mask_mod could and should be cached (e.g. via functools.lru_cache keyed on max_ranges) rather than re-built for every batch. This is a per-forward-step overhead newly introduced for PrefixLM multimodal batches.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 multimodal (image) prefill throughput/latency with mm_prefix enabled on a Blackwell/FA4-capable device; compare steady-state per-step latency with and without mask_mod construction caching.

findings.claim: compute_mm_prefix_range_tensor is now invoked inside each attention metadata builder's build() (flash_attn.py and triton_attn.py) per step, whereas the parent computed the tensor once and shared it across all metadata objects via _set_mm_prefix_range_for_metadata (which explicitly memoized `shared_tensor` to 'avoid redundant host-to-device transfers'). By moving the computation into per-builder build() calls, models with multiple attention groups (e.g. Gemma4's sliding-window + global layer groups) may now perform the H2D transfer and Python range-list construction multiple times per step instead of once, reintroducing the redundant H2D transfers the removed helper was designed to avoid.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 multimodal prefill with multiple attention groups: count H2D transfers / async_tensor_h2d calls per step and compare per-step latency vs the shared-tensor parent implementation.

findings.claim: The new fused torchembed RoPE path in apply_rotary_pos_emb introduces two extra full-tensor layout/copy kernels per call: `t_kernel = t.movedim(0, -2).contiguous()` before the kernel and `t = t_out.movedim(-2, 0).contiguous()` after. `movedim` produces a non-contiguous view, and the explicit `.contiguous()` forces a materializing copy of the entire activation tensor both going in and coming out. This is on the hot path — apply_rotary_pos_emb is called per q/k tensor per attention layer (deepspeed/sequence/layer.py:431-432) and per chunk per q/k in fpdt_layer.py (lines 212/222, called num_chunks times each iteration). The reference path did zero transpose/copy work (pure elementwise `t*cos + rotate_half(t)*sin`). So whenever torchembed is installed + tensor on accelerator + rot_dim even, callers pay two extra transpose-copy passes over the activation that the fused kernel is supposed to save — mirroring the historical unfused-transpose-copy pattern (materializing a permuted tensor before a kernel).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark apply_rotary_pos_emb on CUDA with a realistic [seq_len, batch*heads, dim] tensor (e.g. 4096 x 32 x 128) comparing the reference path vs the torchembed fused path (including the two movedim+contiguous copies) for both latency and peak memory.

findings.claim: The diff inserts MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel into _POSSIBLE_FP8_BLOCK_KERNELS[CUDA] at position 4, AHEAD of TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel. choose_scaled_mm_linear_kernel iterates the (priority/performance-ordered) list and returns the FIRST kernel whose is_supported()&&can_implement() both pass. MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel.can_implement() unconditionally returns (True, None), and its is_supported() on CUDA passes whenever compute_capability < 89 (or VLLM_TEST_FORCE_FP8_MARLIN). Since DeepGemm/Cutlass block kernels are gated (CutlassFp8BlockScaledMMKernel requires CUTLASS_BLOCK_FP8_SUPPORTED and act group_shape=(1,128); DeepGEMM/FlashInfer require their libs), on GPUs where those front kernels are unavailable Marlin will now be selected for block-scaled FP8 and PREEMPT TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel. Marlin FP8 is documented as a compatibility fallback 'for GPUs that lack FP8 hardware support', so selecting it over the Triton block-scaled GEMM can be a throughput regression on affected configs. Because can_implement always returns True, Marlin can never be shadowed by later Triton entry — it strictly wins whenever supported.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Block-scaled FP8 (kFp8Static128BlockSym) linear layer throughput on a GPU with compute_capability < 89 (e.g. Ada/Ampere without CUTLASS block FP8), comparing selection of MarlinFP8ScaledMMLinearKernel vs TritonFp8BlockScaledMMKernel.

The new MLA path in DotProductAttention.forward calls _pad_qkv_head_dim, which issues F.pad on query_layer/key_layer (and value_layer) whenever head_dim_qk != head_dim_v and FA2 is the selected backend. F.pad materializes a fresh, larger (zero-padded) tensor for Q and K every forward call on the hot attention path, plus a corresponding _trim_output reshape/slice on the output. This is per-iteration copy/allocation work introduced to make FA2 accept MLA head dims. It is real and unavoidable given FA2's equal-head-dim constraint, so it is a functional trade-off rather than a clear regression, but it does add a padding-copy on the hot path proportional to the pad amount (e.g. padding qk from 192->256, or v up to qk). Confirm this only fires for the MLA (head_dim_qk != head_dim_v) case and not for standard equal-dim attention.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DotProductAttention forward/backward throughput with MLA head dims (head_dim_qk != head_dim_v, e.g. 192 vs 128) on FA2, comparing padded FA2 path vs fused/unfused fallback

It moves m_splits into a CUDA tensor (avoiding host-side checks t

NVFP4 SReLU backward path adds an explicit BF16 re-quantization step in the wgrad path. When SReLU recompute is enabled and use_nvfp4, the diff replaces the cheap zero-copy GroupedTensor view over the cuDNN dSReLU kernel's already-produced columnwise FP4 data (sfd_col_d_srelu_tensor / d_srelu_tensor) with `fc2_x_bf16 = d_srelu_tensor.view(...).contiguous()` followed by a full `_group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp(fc2_x_bf16, fc2_input_quantizer, ...)`. This materializes a contiguous BF16 copy of the recomputed FC2 input and launches a separate group-quantize kernel per backward pass, on the hot MoE backward path, instead of reusing the fused kernel's output. The MXFP8 branch still uses the zero-copy view; the NVFP4 branch does NOT get the fused output folded in, so this is effectively an un-fused (extra copy + extra quantize kernel) path versus the analogous MXFP8 path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Grouped MLP (MoE) backward with NVFP4 + ScaledSReLU: measure wgrad path latency/extra memory of contiguous() copy + _group_quantize_for_grouped_mlp vs MXFP8 zero-copy view; compare kernel launch count.

findings.claim: The `.contiguous()` on `d_srelu_tensor.view(out_shape[0], fc2_weight_shape[1])` may force an extra allocation+copy of the full recomputed FC2 input activation each backward step if d_srelu_tensor is not already contiguous in the required layout. Combined with the subsequent re-quantize, this doubles the traffic over the recomputed activation compared to consuming the kernel's columnwise FP4 output directly.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark contiguous() copy cost on recomputed FC2 input tensor for representative MoE token counts / hidden sizes.

This diff explicitly FIXES a memory-lifetime issue rather than introducing one. In the parent, backward non-tensor state (quantizers, weakrefs, main_grad closures, saved tensors) lived directly on ctx and survived until ctx GC, extending peak GPU memory under retain_graph=True. The commit's last step nulls out ctx.backward_objects right after _linear_backward so the LinearBwdArgs bag (and everything it references) is released as soon as backward returns. This is a beneficial change, not a regression.

The new gate in quantize() enables the fast specialized cast-only kernel for ROWWISE only when `cols % 128 == 0` (is_full_rowwise_chunk). The specialized rowwise traits actually operate on 32-element chunks (CastTraits::chunkElems = 32, threadLayout Layout<1,32>, warpDimN = 32), so vectorized correctness only requires cols to be a multiple of the vector/chunk granularity (32, or the alignment of uint4 loads), not 128. Requiring cols%128==0 is stricter than necessary and routes common shapes with cols that are multiples of 32/64 but not 128 (e.g. cols=96, 160, 224) back to the slow generic TMA kernel even though the fast kernel could handle them. This is the classic 'fast kernel exists but gating logic rejects it' pattern.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark MXFP8 rowwise cast-only quantize throughput across cols in {96,128,160,192,224,256} x rows large, comparing specialized vs generic kernel to verify whether cols%32==0 (not %128) is the true correctness boundary and how much throughput is left on the table for cols not divisible by 128.

This diff FIXES (does not introduce) a suboptimal-kernel-gate bug in the SM90 FA3-vs-FA4 backend selector. Parent code: `if use_flash_attention_4 and use_flash_attention_3 and device_compute_capability == (9,0): ... use_flash_attention_4 = False`. Here `use_flash_attention_3` is merely the NVTE_FLASH_ATTN env flag (initialized to `use_flash_attention`), NOT a signal that FA3 is actually installed — the `FlashAttentionUtils.v3_is_installed` check is missing. Note the `device_compute_capability != (9,0)` filter only sets `use_flash_attention_3 = False` off-Hopper, so on SM90 the flag stays truthy even when FA3 is not installed. Consequently, on an SM90 machine with FA4 installed but FA3 NOT installed, the parent code would still disable FA4 to 'prefer FA3', but FA3 is unavailable → attention falls back to a slower backend (FA2 varlen / cuDNN-fused) instead of the faster FA4 Hopper kernel. The new condition adds `and FlashAttentionUtils.v3_is_installed` (and `and FlashAttentionUtils.v4_is_installed`), so FA4 is only disabled when FA3 is genuinely available. This restores the fast-kernel path when FA3 is absent. No new regression is introduced by this change.
findings.suggested_benchmark: On SM90 with FA4 installed and FA3 NOT installed, call get_attention_backend() with a standard attention config and assert flash_attention_backend resolves to FA4 (not FA2/cuDNN); measure forward+backward throughput vs the pre-fix build to confirm the FA4 fast path is selected.

The new variable-shape grouped swizzle kernel (grouped_swizzle_scaling_variable_shape_kernel) uses a persistent-grid + device-side block scheduler where EVERY persistent block, on EVERY iteration of the linear-block loop, re-executes a full O(num_tensors) linear scan over m_array/k_array (with round_up_to_multiple / DIVUP arithmetic per tensor) just to map a linear_block_id back to (tensor_id, local_block_id). This is redundant per-iteration work that scales as O(persistent_blocks * total_blocks/gridDim * num_tensors). For large num_tensors (grouped GEMM with many experts) this scan cost, repeated by all blocks, can dominate and make the variable-shape path materially slower than the old per-tensor 3D-grid launch which computed offsets directly from blockIdx.z. A prefix-sum of block counts (computed once, e.g. into shared memory during the warp-reduction phase) with a binary search would remove the per-iteration O(num_tensors) rescan.
findings.suggested_benchmark: grouped swizzle with variable shapes, sweeping num_tensors (e.g. 8/32/128 experts) comparing to uniform-shape fast path

findings.claim: Both the warp-reduction workload computation (first block of the kernel) and the per-iteration linear scan recompute identical per-tensor quantities (padded_m, padded_k, num_tiles_m/k, vec_load_size, grid_dim_x/y) from m_array/k_array. The total_blocks reduction is done once per block but the same arithmetic is duplicated again inside every persistent-loop iteration; the derived per-tensor block counts / prefix sums are not cached in shared memory, so the divide/round-up work is repeated many times per block on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: micro-benchmark variable-shape grouped swizzle kernel time vs uniform path at fixed total work, varying num_tensors

In the new AITER hc_head wrapper (vllm/_aiter_ops.py rocm_aiter_ops.hc_head), every call allocates three fresh device tensors — full_fn (shape hc_mult3 x hc_mult*hidden_size, e.g. 24 x 4*hidden_size fp32), full_base (hc_mult3 fp32) and full_scale (3 fp32) — via torch.zeros(...) and then copies the (constant, weight-derived) fn/hc_base/hc_scale into their leading slices before calling mhc_pre. Because fn/hc_base/hc_scale are model parameters that never change between calls, this padding buffer is rebuilt (zero + slice-copy) on every hc_head invocation on the ROCm hot path. The full_fn zero-fill+copy is the dominant cost: it zeroes and writes an fp32 buffer of size hc_mult3 * hc_mult * hidden_size each token step. This matches the historical 'wasted zero/copy work on a correctly-sized buffer allocated per forward step' pattern — the padded fn/base/scale should be precomputed once at load time (or the AITER call restructured to not require padding) rather than re-materialized every call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbench rocm_aiter_ops.hc_head on ROCm/DSV4 decode: compare per-call latency with per-call full_fn/full_base/full_scale construction vs. a version where the padded buffers are precomputed once and reused.

The new RoutedExpertsManager (scheduler-side CPU buffer) is sized to the ENTIRE physical block pool: max_num_slots = kv_cache_config.num_blocks * block_size, shape (max_num_slots, num_hidden_layers, num_experts_per_tok). By the code's own admission this 'can reach multiple GB' of always-resident host RAM per DP rank. The previous shared-memory design sized the host buffer to max_num_kv_tokens = (num_blocks // num_groups) * min_block_size (a per-group slice), whereas the new code sizes to num_blocks * block_size (full pool, not divided by num_groups). This is a memory-vs-simplicity tradeoff that materially increases steady-state CPU memory footprint for large KV pools. It is one-time allocation (init), not monotonic growth, so it is a footprint tradeoff rather than a leak.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure host RSS of the scheduler/engine-core process at startup with enable_return_routed_experts on a large-block-pool config (e.g. num_blocks ~ 100k+, many layers) vs off; compare against the pre-change shared-memory buffer sizing (num_blocks//num_groups * min_block_size).

findings.claim: store_batch performs `self.routed_experts_by_slot[slot_mapping] = data` once per scheduler step. This is a numpy fancy-index (advanced indexing) assignment over (num_scheduled_tokens, num_layers, top_k) with an implicit dtype narrowing (int32 -> uint8/uint16). Fancy-index assignment forces a gather/scatter copy plus a dtype cast every step on the scheduler process (a CPU-bound serial hot path). For large batches x many layers this is 'few MB per step' of per-step CPU copy that did not exist as a per-step cost in the same place before. Confirm it is on the every-step scheduler update_from_output path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark scheduler update_from_output wall time per step with enable_return_routed_experts on vs off at max_num_batched_tokens, for a deep MoE model (many hidden layers), measuring the store_batch fancy-index+cast cost.

findings.claim: clip_qk() in megatron/core/optimizer/qk_clip.py issues one torch.distributed.all_reduce(op=MAX) per attention layer, iterating over every transformer layer of every model chunk each optimizer step. This is a per-layer collective launched serially inside the (timed) optimizer step. With N decoder layers this becomes N small all_reduces over the DP+CP group every iteration, each of tiny payload (num_heads floats). Many tiny back-to-back collectives are latency-bound and cannot overlap, and unlike gradient reductions they are not bucketed/fused. On deep models this adds measurable per-step synchronization overhead to the critical training path whenever --qk-clip or --log-max-attention-logit is enabled.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Train a deep model (e.g. 40+ layers) with --qk-clip enabled and measure per-iteration optimizer time vs a run with qk_clip disabled; compare against a fused single all_reduce over stacked per-layer max logits.

findings.claim: clip_qk() calls .item() on the reduced max-logit tensor per layer each optimizer step, forcing a device-to-host synchronization for every attention layer. Combined with the per-layer all_reduce, these repeated syncs serialize the step and prevent kernel-launch pipelining. Consider gathering all per-layer maxima into one tensor, doing a single all_reduce, and a single .item()/.max() at the end.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure optimizer-step wall time with per-layer .item()+all_reduce vs a batched single-collective/single-sync implementation on a deep model.

The diff adds `and self.weight.dtype == torch.bfloat16` to `allow_dsv3_router_gemm` in gate_linear.py. Combined with `_get_moe_router_dtype()` returning fp32 for `model_type == 'glm_moe_dsa'` (GLM-5/5.2), the gate is now constructed with params_dtype=fp32 (force_fp32_compute=True keeps weights fp32). For the GLM shape (hidden_size=6144, n_routed_experts=256), which IS in DSV3_SUPPORTED_HIDDEN_SIZES/NUM_EXPERTS and previously dispatched to the dedicated `dsv3_router_gemm` specialized kernel (added by PR #46385 'GLM5 Router GEMM'), the new bf16-weight guard now disables Tier 1. Since (6144,256) is not in FP32_SUPPORTED_SHAPES ({(3072,256),(6144,128)}) Tier 2 is unavailable, and the cuBLAS Tier 3 requires bf16 weight so it is unavailable. The router GEMM therefore falls all the way through to the generic Tier-4 F.linear path with fp32 weights, bypassing the just-merged GLM5 router kernel. On the router hot path (every decode/prefill step) this is a latency/throughput regression for GLM-5.2 relative to what the specialized kernel provided.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-bench GateLinear.forward for shape (M<=16, H=6144, E=256) comparing fp32-weight F.linear fallback vs the dsv3_router_gemm specialized kernel; end-to-end GLM-5.2 decode tokens/s.

findings.claim: Setting params_dtype=fp32 for the GLM gate stores the router weight (hidden_size x n_experts = 6144x256) in fp32 instead of bf16, doubling the resident router weight memory and forcing an fp32 input activation copy (`x.to(self.weight.dtype)`) on every router call in the Tier-4 fallback. The activation upcast materializes an fp32 copy of the [num_tokens, 6144] hidden states per layer per step. This is intended for numerical correctness of fp32 routing, but is a genuine memory/bandwidth increase versus bf16 routing; note it is the inverse of the historical `low-precision-state` prior (this widens dtype rather than narrows it).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare peak memory and per-step router latency for GLM-5.2 with fp32 vs bf16 routing.

findings.claim: The new FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel is inserted at the TOP (highest priority) of _POSSIBLE_NVFP4_KERNELS[PlatformEnum.CUDA], ahead of the previously-default FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel. Its is_supported() only checks sm_10x + has_flashinfer() and can_implement() unconditionally returns (True, None), so on all SM100 devices it will silently become the auto-selected default NVFP4 GEMM backend for every quantized linear layer. This is a default backend swap identical to the historical fused-backend-swap pattern (FlashInfer TRTLLM/CUTLASS defaulting change). If the cute-dsl kernel is not universally faster than cutlass across all shapes/batch sizes, this regresses throughput/latency by default for existing SM100 NVFP4 deployments — with no shape/heuristic gating to fall back to cutlass.
findings.suggested_benchmark: NVFP4 linear-layer GEMM microbench on SM100 comparing cute-dsl vs cutlass across (M in {1,8,64,512,4096}, typical N/K) plus end-to-end decode/prefill throughput before/after this default swap.

findings.claim: FlashInferCuteDslNvFp4LinearKernel does NOT override input_quant_key() (base returns None), unlike FlashInferCutlassNvFp4LinearKernel which returns kNvfp4Dynamic and accepts a pre-quantized QuantizedActivation via as_quantized_activation(). As a result apply_weights() always calls scaled_fp4_quant(x, ...) inline on the hot path every forward, even when the activation was already quantized upstream (e.g. by the FlashInferAllGatherFP4Pattern fusion this same PR registers for 'cute-dsl' in collective_fusion.py). Since this kernel is now the auto-selected default, the fused/reused activation-quant path is bypassed and activation re-quantization is done redundantly per layer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare per-token latency of an NVFP4 linear layer under cute-dsl backend with and without a fused pre-quantized activation input, and verify the AllGather+quant fusion actually elides the in-apply scaled_fp4_quant call.

findings.claim: 此改动触及 redundant-hot-path-work（host-overhead），该类问题历史上主要在 [distributed optimizer (use_distributed_optimizer=True); overhead scales with data_parallel_world_size and number of buckets, multi-GPU with model-parallel / distributed optimizer (extra all_reduce over grad_stats_parallel_group); ChainedOptimizer used e.g. with MoE+dense hybrid, TP (uses tensor_model_parallel_world_size)] 配置下显现；这些改动把 torch.zeros 换成 torch.empty（以及移除 z_out.zero_()），目的是消除张量分配后额外的填零 kernel/内存写入。填零是一次在热路径上多余的写工作，去掉它减少了冗余的 GPU 工作量。这类"移除张量分配后的额外初始化"最贴近 redundant-hot-path-work

findings.claim: The new Realtime WebSocket transcription path (OpenAIServingRealtime / RealtimeConnection) is a fresh audio-transcription entrypoint that does NOT reuse the cold-start warmup that the existing HTTP transcription path (OpenAISpeechToText.__init__) already performs via _warmup_audio_preprocessing() and _warmup_input_processor(). Those warmups exist precisely because the first librosa/mel-spectrogram call triggers FFTW + numba JIT (~7s) and the first multimodal input_processor.process_inputs triggers multimodal init (~2.5s). If OpenAIServingRealtime does not invoke equivalent warmups at init, the first realtime WebSocket utterance will pay this multi-second cold-start cost on the hot streaming path, defeating the low-latency purpose of the Realtime API. Verify serving.py's __init__ calls the warmup helpers (or inherits them) and that audio decode/resample uses a pre-warmed path.

In MHCPreOp.forward_hip / MHCPostOp.forward_hip (ROCm path), the diff comments out the fused AITER kernel dispatch (torch.ops.vllm.mhc_pre_aiter / mhc_post_aiter, used when hidden_size % 256 == 0) and forces the reference PyTorch implementation (mhc_kernels.mhc_pre_torch / mhc_post_torch) unconditionally. mhc_pre_torch/mhc_post_torch are multi-op elementwise implementations (RMS norm + sigmoid mixes + weighted residual sums via several separate torch ops), whereas the AITER path is a single fused kernel. These ops run once per DeepSeek V4 decoder layer per forward (hc_pre/hc_post invoked per layer in DeepseekV4DecoderLayer.forward), so this is a hot path. Disabling the fused backend introduces a ROCm throughput/latency regression versus the parent. This is the inverse of the historical fused-backend-swap pattern (removing rather than adding a prebuilt fused library kernel). The change is intentional for correctness (accuracy problem at large token counts, pending an aiter bugfix), but the perf cost should be noted.
findings.suggested_benchmark: DeepSeek V4 decode/prefill throughput on ROCm with hidden_size % 256 == 0 (AITER enabled), comparing parent (AITER mhc_pre/post) vs this commit (torch fallback).

In `get_encode_kwargs`, the change broadens the condition that disables tokenizer-level truncation. Previously only `truncation_side == 'left'` disabled HF-level truncation (passing `truncation=False`); now any request with `truncation_side is not None and truncate_prompt_tokens is not None` disables it — including `truncation_side='right'` cases that previously used `truncation=True, max_length=N`. When tokenizer-level truncation is disabled, `tokenizer.encode` must tokenize the *entire* prompt string and produce the full token-id list, after which `_token_truncation` slices it in Python (`tokens[:max_length]`). For a request explicitly asking to keep only N tokens out of a very long prompt, this makes encoding cost scale with the full prompt length instead of the truncation length. This is on the per-request tokenization path (`_tokenize_prompt` / `_tokenize_prompt_async`). Because this only triggers when the caller explicitly opts in to `truncation_side`, the impact is bounded to those requests, but it is a real added per-request cost vs the prior right-truncation fast path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Tokenize a very long prompt (e.g. 100k chars) with truncate_prompt_tokens=8 and truncation_side='right' vs 'left'/unset; compare encode latency to confirm full-sequence tokenization overhead.

findings.claim: The change moves cudaHostRegister pinning off the init path into a background daemon-less thread (self.pin_thread) that is only join()ed at shutdown(). This makes worker init faster, but transfers via transfer_async() / _swap_blocks_batch can now begin while the pin thread is still running and BEFORE cudaHostRegister has completed. During that window DMA copies read/write from *unregistered* (unpinned) host memory, giving a slower unpinned DMA path (extra pageable-buffer staging inside the driver). The parent code guaranteed pinning finished synchronously before any transfer. There is no wait/gate (no join, no is_pinned check) on the hot transfer path, so early offload/load requests silently run degraded until pinning finishes. Recommend gating the first transfer on pin_thread completion (or documenting/accepting the cold-start slow window).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure H2D/D2H offload bandwidth for the first N transfers immediately after worker init with the background-pin change vs synchronous pinning; verify no sustained unpinned-DMA slowdown.

findings.claim: In cpu_fused_moe.py the SILU entry of `_CPU_MOE_ACT_FN` was changed from a bare static-method reference `SiluAndMul.forward_native` to `lambda x: SiluAndMul(compile_native=False).forward_native(x)`. This dict entry is invoked per-expert inside `cpu_fused_moe_torch` (`gate_up = _CPU_MOE_ACT_FN[act](gate_up)`), which runs once for every active expert on every MoE forward on the CPU torch path. The old version simply called an unbound static method with zero construction cost; the new lambda constructs a fresh `SiluAndMul` CustomOp on *every* activation call. `CustomOp.__init__` calls `dispatch_forward`, which calls `get_cached_compilation_config()`, mutates `compilation_config.enabled_custom_ops/disabled_custom_ops` sets, and calls `nn.Module.__init__`. That is substantial per-call Python overhead (nn.Module init + config lookup + set update) added to a hot per-expert loop, versus a trivial function pointer before. This is a per-call heavy-object-construction regression on the CPU MoE hot path.

In gpu_ubatch_wrapper.py __call__(), `num_tokens = (ubatch_slices[0].token_slice.stop - ubatch_slices[0].token_slice.start) * 2` remains hardcoded to `* 2` and is NOT updated for num_ubatches. This value is the cudagraph key (self.cudagraphs[num_tokens]) and the total token count. When ubatch_size > 2 (the new XBO path), num_tokens is computed as first_ubatch_tokens*2 instead of the true total (~ first_ubatch_tokens*num_ubatches). This mis-keys the ubatched cudagraph cache: capture stores under a wrong num_tokens, and the replay-vs-capture branch (`num_tokens in self.cudagraphs`) will miss, forcing repeated re-capture / falling back to eager _run_ubatches instead of replaying the full-graph. The overlapped fast path silently degrades to non-graph execution, defeating the compute/comm overlap the feature exists to provide.

The workspace resize path in `_ensure_workspace_size` calls `torch.accelerator.empty_cache()` whenever the requested size exceeds the current workspace. Continuation-prefill dequant buffer size is `ceil(cached_len/block_size)*block_size` which GROWS monotonically with context length. On the FIRST forward at each new (larger) context length, `get_simultaneous` triggers a resize → `empty_cache()`, a device-wide CUDA caching-allocator flush that stalls the stream. Under long-context decode the buffer grows repeatedly, so this pays a full allocator flush many times before the workspace stabilizes / is locked. Previously the per-layer buffer grew independently and used plain `torch.empty` (no empty_cache flush).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Long-context prefill ramp (2K→8K→32K) measuring per-step latency spikes on first request at each new max length

findings.claim: The auto-selection logic in ParallelConfig now defaults async EPLB (eplb_config.use_async, communicator==None) to the 'torch_gloo' backend. Previously, for the default all2all backend 'allgather_reducescatter', async EPLB was force-disabled (use_async=False), so the expert-weight P2P exchange ran synchronously over GPU NCCL. With the new default, all async EPLB traffic is routed through TorchDistGlooStagedEplbCommunicator, which stages every expert weight tensor through CPU (D2H copy), transfers over the gloo (TCP/CPU) backend instead of NCCL/NVLink, then copies back H2D. This CPU-staged, gloo-based path has substantially lower bandwidth than direct GPU-to-GPU NCCL send/recv, so the default configuration silently chooses a slower communication path for expert weight rebalancing. This matches the config-toggle-perf-feature mechanism: the auto-selected default value routes onto a slow path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure EPLB rebalance transfer latency per layer with async EPLB + allgather_reducescatter comparing communicator='torch_gloo' (new default) vs 'torch_nccl' on a multi-GPU MoE model.

findings.claim: In vllm/envs.py use_aot_compile(), the default gate for AOT compile was RAISED from is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.10.0.dev") to is_torch_equal_or_newer("2.11.0.dev"). This PR upgrades the pinned torch to 2.10.0 final, so on the newly-shipped torch 2.10.0 the previous behavior (VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE default = "1") now flips OFF because 2.10.0 < 2.11.0.dev. Every other version guard in this same PR (compiler_interface.py, decorators.py, batch_invariant.py) was migrated from "2.10.0.dev" to "2.10.0" so that AOT/standalone-compile features light up on 2.10.0 final — but use_aot_compile went the other direction, disabling the AOT-compile fast path by default on the very torch version this release targets. This is the config-toggle-perf-feature pattern: the static default now selects the slower (non-AOT) compilation path at startup for the default install. If unintentional, it regresses torch.compile startup/warm-load time for all default 2.10.0 users.

findings.claim: The diff statically flips `enable_flashinfer_autotune` from True to False in OPTIMIZATION_LEVEL_01/_02/_03 in vllm/config/vllm.py. This is the default optimization level (O2), so out-of-the-box deployments on Hopper (SM 9.0) / Blackwell (SM 10.0) GPUs with FlashInfer installed will no longer run `flashinfer_autotune()` during kernel_warmup (kernel_warmup.py branches on this flag). Per the code's own docstring, without autotuning 'FlashInfer will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower' for the selected kernel implementations — a genuine runtime throughput/latency regression across all runs on affected hardware. The commit intentionally accepts this slowdown as a temporary correctness workaround (linked flashinfer-ai/flashinfer#3197), so it is a deliberate correctness-forces-slow-path tradeoff rather than an accidental misfire; the perf cost is nonetheless real and should be reverted once the upstream correctness bug is fixed.
findings.suggested_benchmark: End-to-end decode/prefill throughput and TTFT/ITL on H100/B200 with FlashInfer backend at default -O2, comparing autotune enabled vs disabled (e.g. serving benchmark on a dense model at high concurrency).

findings.claim: The batching optimization only batches the encoder (patch_embedder + encoder) calls. The pooling stage is still executed in a per-image / per-frame Python loop that reslices each item (single_hidden = last_hidden_states[i].unsqueeze(0); single_pos_ids = pixel_position_ids[i].unsqueeze(0); vt.pooler(...)). This iterates one item at a time with .unsqueeze(0)/indexing per iteration, launching per-item pooler kernels and Python overhead proportional to the number of images/frames. For video with up to 32 frames per video across a batch, this is a hot per-frame loop that partially defeats the stated batching goal and keeps the O(N) kernel-launch cost. Same historical shape as the serial-per-batch-element loop (matmul_persistent(a[i], b[i]) in a Python for-loop) that the kernel-fusion prior flags.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 vision encode latency with N images (N=8,16,32) and multi-frame video (32 frames), measuring _process_image_input / _process_video_input wall time and pooler kernel-launch count.

findings.claim: In both _process_image_input and _process_video_input the code re-materializes the encoder inputs via torch.cat of per-item unsqueezed slices: pv_tensor = torch.cat([item[1].unsqueeze(0) for item in chunk_items], dim=0) (and pp_tensor similarly), plus torch.cat over per-item last_hidden_states_list and per-item pooled valid_states. When the input already arrives as a stacked contiguous tensor (same-resolution batch), these torch.cat calls allocate a fresh full-size copy of the pixel_values / hidden_states instead of slicing views. This is wasted copy work on correctly-sized data that grows with total patches * hidden_size (potentially large for high-resolution image batches).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Memory allocation and latency for same-resolution image batch (single stacked tensor) vs mixed-resolution list, isolating the torch.cat copies in _process_image_input.

findings.claim: In the hot sampling path `_gumbel_sample_kernel`, the logits are now cast to FP64 (`logits = logits.to(tl.float64)`) over the full [num_tokens, vocab_size] tensor. All downstream vocab-wide work — the `tl.where` masking, the `-tl.log(-tl.log(u))` transform, and especially the `tl.max(..., return_indices=True)` reduction — now executes in FP64. On the GPUs vLLM commonly targets (A100/H100 non-DGX, consumer cards), FP64 throughput is 1/32–1/64 of FP32, so this widens the arithmetic-bound region of the per-decode-step sampling kernel over vocab sizes of 128K–256K. This is a per-step decode hot path (called for every non-greedy request each token), so the widening directly adds decode latency. The switch from the prior FP32 implementation (commit 5fcb0cdd6 'Use FP32 for Gumbel Noise') to FP64 is a precision-motivated change whose GPU cost should be benchmarked.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-step sampling microbenchmark: gumbel_sample over [num_tokens, vocab_size] with vocab 128K/256K on A100/H100, comparing this FP64 kernel vs the parent FP32 kernel latency.

findings.claim: `local_max` in gumbel_sample was changed from float32 to float64 (`logits.new_empty(num_tokens, num_blocks, dtype=torch.float64)`). This doubles the intermediate reduction buffer size and the memory-bandwidth of the follow-up `local_max.argmax(dim=-1)` / `gather`. Minor vs the kernel-internal FP64 cost, but contributes to the overall widening on the hot path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure host-side gumbel_sample end-to-end latency including the argmax/gather over the widened float64 local_max buffer.

findings.claim: In DFlashQwen3Model.precompute_and_store_context_kv (called every draft step / forward pass), K/V are processed with per-layer Python for-loops: `for i in range(L): ops.rms_norm(all_k_normed[i], ...)` and `for i in range(L): attn = self._attn_layers[i]; attn.impl.do_kv_cache_update(...)`. Because context shapes differ from query shapes, this path is explicitly excluded from torch.compile/CUDA-graph (per the docstring), so these L eager per-layer kernel launches (rms_norm + do_kv_cache_update) execute on the hot path each step, incurring per-launch Python/CPU overhead that scales with num_hidden_layers. Consider fusing per-layer rms_norm into one batched call (like the fused KV GEMM already done) and batching the cache writes.

This diff force-sets `scheduler_config.disable_hybrid_kv_cache_manager = True` whenever `kv_transfer_config is not None` (any KV connector: NIXL, LMCache, shared-storage, CPU offload, MultiConnector). This unconditionally turns OFF the hybrid KV cache manager (HMA allocator), forcing the uniform (non-hybrid) KV cache path for ALL connector deployments — even for connectors that DO support HMA (subclasses of `SupportsHMA`). Previously, HMA was only disabled/errored for connectors that don't support it: the factory (`create_connector`) computed `hma_enabled = not disable_hybrid_kv_cache_manager` and raised only when `hma_enabled and not supports_hma(connector_cls)`, letting HMA-capable connectors keep the hybrid allocator. This is a static config toggle choosing the slow/less-efficient path regardless of connector capability, matching the config-toggle-perf-feature pattern. Impact: for models with sliding-window or Mamba attention, disabling HMA yields less efficient (uniform) KV block allocation — higher memory footprint and reduced throughput, as the warning message itself acknowledges.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Throughput/memory of a sliding-window model (e.g. Gemma-2) or Mamba model under a KV connector that IS a SupportsHMA subclass, comparing HMA-on (pre-diff, via factory guard) vs forced HMA-off (post-diff). Measure tokens/s and peak KV cache memory / max concurrent sequences.

findings.claim: The condition uses a coarse `kv_transfer_config is not None` predicate rather than checking connector capability (`supports_hma(connector_cls)`) that already exists in KVConnectorFactory.create_connector. Because the check does not consult the connector's SupportsHMA status, HMA-capable connectors are wrongly routed to the non-hybrid fast-path fallback. A capability-aware guard would preserve the efficient allocator for connectors that support it; the current change is deliberately conservative for stability but over-broad. Recommend gating on connector HMA support instead of a blanket disable.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Compare allocation efficiency with an HMA-supporting connector when disable is gated on supports_hma() vs the blanket disable.

findings.claim: In AiterFlashAttentionMetadataBuilder.build (hot per-forward path), the change `seq_lens = common_attn_metadata.seq_lens_cpu` -> `seq_lens = common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.cpu()` replaces a *cached* CPU shadow with an unconditional fresh device->host copy. The deprecated `seq_lens_cpu` property lazily reuses `_seq_lens_cpu`, which the runner already pre-populates from a pinned/non_blocking CPU copy (see gpu_model_runner.py:1644 `_seq_lens_cpu=self.seq_lens.cpu[...]` and gpu/attn_utils.py:171). When that shadow exists, the old code performed NO synchronization; `.cpu()` always forces a blocking GPU->CPU sync on every build() call, stalling on all previously-enqueued GPU work. Because build() runs once per model forward, this reintroduces a per-step D2H sync on the ROCm AITER FA path.

findings.claim: execute_in_parallel launches aux GEMMs (compressor kv_score, indexer.weights_proj, indexer.compressor kv_score) on separate aux streams, each allocating a fresh output tensor via the caching allocator on the aux stream, then joins with done_events[i].wait() on the current/default stream and returns those tensors to be consumed on the default stream (qr_kv.split, compressor(kv_score...), indexer(..., indexer_kv_score, indexer_weights,...)). None of the aux-stream-allocated results have record_stream(current_stream) called on them. The event wait establishes the compute ordering, but the caching allocator only tracks which stream an allocation is safe to be freed/reused on via record_stream; without it, once the Python ref to an aux result is dropped the block can be reused by a later allocation on the default stream while the aux stream still (logically) owns it, i.e. a cross-stream allocator reuse hazard analogous to the shared-experts clone-in-aux-stream race in #26440/344a0017c.

findings.claim: The new `can_fit_full_sequence` admission gate is invoked on the per-request scheduling loop (scheduler.py ~line 725) immediately before `allocate_slots`. It calls `coordinator.get_num_blocks_to_allocate(...)` for the FULL sequence length, which fans out across every single_type_manager (and every kv_cache_group in hybrid/multi-group models). `allocate_slots` then re-runs `get_num_blocks_to_allocate` a second time for the chunk. Because `scheduler_reserve_full_isl` defaults to True, this doubling of the per-request block-accounting computation is unconditionally added to the hot scheduling path for all users, not just those who opt in. For hybrid/many-group models and large ISL this is a measurable per-step overhead (extra loop over managers plus min() clamps per admitted/attempted request).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Scheduler-only microbenchmark: many waiting requests with large prompts on a hybrid KV-cache model, measure schedule() latency with scheduler_reserve_full_isl True vs False.

findings.claim: Parent-side weight prefetch in _startup_prefetch_weights reads ALL safetensors/bin shards into page cache from the APIServer parent, while the existing in-child prefetch (_prefetch_all_checkpoints in weight_utils.py) also reads the same files, and crucially shards reads by rank (paths_to_prefetch = sorted_files[rank::world_size]). The parent prefetch reads the FULL set (no rank sharding) using an 8-thread pool. On a warm/fast disk or when weights already fit in RAM, this duplicates I/O that the child would do anyway, and on tens-of-GB models the parent thread can still be actively reading shards when the child forks and begins its own concurrent reads of the same files, causing read contention / double page-cache pressure rather than a clean head-start. This is the redundant-load-startup-work mechanism: duplicated data work after processes exist. The guard only skips headless API-only workers (input_address), not the common single-server case where child prefetch fully overlaps.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Cold-cache vs warm-cache `vllm serve` startup for a ~30GB model: compare weight-load phase wall time with parent prefetch enabled vs disabled; also measure disk read bytes to detect double reads and contention when parent+child read concurrently.

findings.claim: The per-parameter validation in register_z3_param performs multiple small GPU allocations and per-rank D2H copies inside a tight loop: torch::tensor({padded_per_rank}, cuda) H2D, torch::empty_like per rank (world_size allocations), then a per-rank .to(torch::kCPU).item<int64_t>() which is a blocking device synchronize. All of this correctly-sized-but-unnecessary work is executed once per parameter purely to verify a value that is already deterministically computable on every rank, wasting allocation/copy/sync cycles proportional to num_params * world_size.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile allocation count and cudaMemcpy/synchronize calls during parameter registration for a model with many parameters; count D2H item() syncs.

findings.claim: In register_z3_param (csrc/compile/z3.cpp), the diff adds a full ProcessGroup::allgather(...)->wait() collective for EVERY registered parameter, executed at init time in the loop `for p in engine.module.parameters(): dc.register_z3_param(...)` (deepspeed/compile/init_z3.py). This introduces O(num_params) blocking synchronization barriers during model initialization. For large models with thousands of parameters, this adds thousands of serialized device-CPU round-trips (each all_padded_counts[i].to(torch::kCPU).item<int64_t>() forces a GPU->CPU sync + separate CUDA H2D copies via torch::tensor on CUDA) plus a full collective wait per parameter, significantly slowing DeepCompile startup. The check is purely a defensive validation (all ranks compute padded_per_rank identically from ds_shape and world_size), so the collective is essentially redundant verification work rather than functional necessity.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure init_z3 / model registration wall-clock for a model with a large number of parameters (e.g. thousands of tensors) at world_size>=2, comparing parent vs this commit; watch for per-parameter collective+D2H sync overhead.

The new `input_fits_in_drafter` guard added in this diff calls `spec_decode_common_attn_metadata.seq_lens.max()`. In CommonAttentionMetadata, `seq_lens` is the GPU device tensor (populated from `self.seq_lens = self._make_buffer(...)`, i.e. the `.gpu` half of a CpuGpuBuffer via `self.seq_lens.copy_(self.seq_lens_cpu, non_blocking=True)`), while the host-resident copy lives in `seq_lens_cpu`. Calling `.max()` on the device tensor and then comparing it against a Python int forces a blocking GPU->CPU synchronization on the hot per-step decode path. This defeats the async/overlapped EAGLE padded-batch path that was specifically designed to use GPU sampled tokens without waiting for bookkeeping, reintroducing a device-to-host stall every decode step. It should read `seq_lens_cpu.max()` instead.

findings.claim: In _apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd, CASE 2 (max-seqlen freqs format) is reverted from a single batched RoPE application to a per-sequence Python loop. Parent code built one packed freqs tensor via torch.cat and called _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd ONCE on the whole packed tensor t. The new code loops over sequence_splits and calls _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd once PER packed sequence, plus an extra output.narrow(...).copy_(output_slice) per sequence into a preallocated buffer. Since _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd internally launches several elementwise kernels (torch.cos, torch.sin, _rotate_half's chunk/cat, the fused multiply-add, and a final torch.cat), this multiplies the kernel-launch count by the number of packed sequences and adds N copy_ kernels. On this unfused RoPE fallback (apply_rope_fusion=False), invoked per attention layer per forward step, this is a throughput regression proportional to the packing factor. This exactly matches the historical THD RoPE case where the prior commit had moved both branches to build a single packed tensor and 'apply once'; this diff undoes that for CASE 2. The change is motivated by a correctness fix (sequences must not be treated as continuations), but the per-sequence loop form is the perf cost; a vectorized alternative (e.g. building per-sequence freqs with per-sequence offsets and still applying once) would avoid the regression.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Micro-benchmark _apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd with CASE-2 (max-seqlen) freqs and a packed batch of many short sequences (e.g. cu_seqlens for 32 sequences of len 128), cp_size=1 and cp_size>1, comparing kernel-launch count / wall time vs the parent single-apply implementation.

Gemma4MTPMaskedEmbedder.forward() allocates a full-vocab dense tensor of shape (num_tokens, vocab_size) filled with -inf every call via torch.full, then scatters sparse logits into it. This runs per draft step on the hot decode path and defeats the purpose of the sparse centroid selection (which only computes ~top_k * vocab/num_centroids entries). When compute_logits() takes this masked_embedding path, a full-vocab allocation + fill dominates. The intended sparse path (get_top_tokens) avoids this, but compute_logits() still materializes the dense tensor whenever masked_embedding is enabled.

findings.claim: `_has_module` was changed from a cheap metadata lookup (`importlib.util.find_spec(module_name) is not None`) to also performing a full `importlib.import_module(module_name)` trial import. For heavy optional native deps this executes the module's full top-level import (loading shared libs, initializing CUDA/backends, running side-effectful module init) rather than just checking availability. Because it is used by many `has_*` helpers (`has_deep_gemm`, `has_deep_ep`, `has_aiter`, `has_triton_kernels`, `has_fbgemm_gpu`, `has_cutedsl`, `has_tilelang`, `has_mori`, `has_helion`, `has_arctic_inference`), the first call to any of these now pays the cost of fully importing potentially large packages (e.g. deep_gemm, aiter, fbgemm_gpu, triton_kernels), inflating startup/first-use latency compared to `find_spec` which never imports.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure cold-start time of `has_deep_gemm()`/`has_aiter()`/`has_triton_kernels()` (first, uncached call) before vs after the change in an env where these native packages are installed; compare `find_spec`-only vs trial-import latency.

findings.claim: The trial import means availability probes now trigger the target module's import-time side effects even when the caller only wanted to know if it exists (e.g. building capability tables, `@pytest.mark.skipif(not has_deep_gemm())` guards, `has_deep_gemm() and comm_sms > 0` in gpu_ubatch_wrapper). This can eagerly initialize backends/CUDA contexts earlier than the LazyLoader design intends, which may pull large dependencies at import time. Result is @cache'd so it is a one-time-per-module cost, not per-call.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Trace which modules get eagerly imported during vLLM engine startup when any has_* helper is consulted; verify no large native package is imported earlier than before.

In DeepseekV4MLP.__init__, when block-FP8 quant is active and not sequence-parallel, intermediate_size is rounded up to n_local*block_size[0]*tp_size (cdiv). This padded intermediate dimension is baked into the shared-expert gate_up_proj/down_proj layers, so every forward pass computes GEMMs and SiluAndMul over up to ~(block_size[0]*tp_size - 1) extra zero-filled rows per rank. The extra FLOPs/memory are incurred on every decode/prefill step, not just at load. The overhead is bounded (at most ~one 128-block per rank) and is required to make TEP=16 block-aligned (a previously-unsupported config), so this is an acceptable enablement tradeoff rather than a regression on an existing working path — but reviewers should be aware the shared expert now runs slightly wider matmuls at runtime.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Decode-latency / shared-expert forward microbench with block-FP8 DeepSeek-V4 at TP=16 comparing intermediate sizes that are vs. are not a multiple of 128*16, to quantify padded-GEMM overhead.

findings.claim: In serve_tokens_stream_generator (streaming path uses RequestOutputKind.DELTA), the new routed_experts encoding runs once per streaming delta chunk (i.e. per decode step), not once at completion. Each invocation allocates a fresh io.BytesIO(), calls np.save (which builds a full .npy header + writes the array), then base64-encodes. When enable_return_routed_experts=True, this fixed per-call overhead recurs on every token in the decode hot loop, adding CPU work to the streaming path proportional to output length. This is opt-in (guarded by 'output.routed_experts is not None'), so impact is limited to MoE routed-experts capture runs, but it is a genuine per-token cost that did not exist before on the stream path. Consider batching/accumulating routed_experts and encoding once, or reusing a buffer.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming /inference/v1/generate with enable_return_routed_experts=True on an MoE model; measure inter-token latency (ITL/TPOT) and API-server CPU vs. baseline (feature disabled) at long output lengths.

findings.claim: gemma4_mm.py adds `_clear_mm_prefix_for_full_attn_layers()` invoked on every forward() call before the language model. It calls get_forward_context() and, when full-attn layer set is non-empty, iterates the entire attn_metadata dict (all layers), parsing each layer_name with `.split('.layers.')[1].split('.')[0]` + `int(...)` and a hasattr check per layer. This is O(num_layers) string parsing per forward step on the decode hot path for Gemma4 bidi='vision' models. The early-return `if not self._full_attn_layer_idxs` guards non-bidi models, but for bidi models this per-step string/regex-style parsing over all layers is exactly the redundant-hot-path-work pattern (per-call parsing that could be precomputed to a layer_name set). The docstring claims it avoids per-call regex via precomputed idxs, but per-call it still re-parses every layer_name string each step.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Gemma4 (bidirectional='vision') decode-step latency vs layer count; measure per-step overhead of _clear_mm_prefix_for_full_attn_layers

findings.claim: In _get_reqs_to_store (called once per scheduler step for every running/cached request via build_connector_meta), the touch call was changed from bounded slicing `self.manager.touch(group_state.offload_keys[:num_blocks])` to touching the ENTIRE accumulated key list for every group: `for group_state in req_status.group_states: self.manager.touch(group_state.offload_keys)`. offload_keys grows monotonically with the request's decoded length (via update_offload_keys appending each step), so touch() now scans/reorders O(total_blocks_so_far) entries per request per scheduler step instead of O(new_blocks). LRUCachePolicy.touch iterates `reversed(list(keys))` and does move_to_end per key, so the per-step cost grows linearly with sequence length -> O(seq^2) aggregate touch work over the lifetime of a long request. This is a repeated hot-path regression, not one-time work.

findings.claim: The added `@model_serializer(mode="wrap")` on `DeltaMessage` (vllm/entrypoints/openai/engine/protocol.py) runs on EVERY streaming chunk serialization. In chat_completion/serving.py, each streamed chunk calls `chunk.model_dump_json(exclude_unset=True)` (lines ~533, 569, 751), which recursively invokes this custom serializer per DeltaMessage per token. This wraps the fast pydantic-core serialization with a Python-level callback that builds `data`, does `data.get(...)`, and `data.pop(...)` for every chunk in the hot streaming path. For high token-throughput streaming this adds a small but per-token Python overhead. Note: with `exclude_unset=True` and `tool_calls` using `default_factory=list`, unset tool_calls would already be excluded, so the serializer's benefit is only for the explicitly-set-empty case; but the Python callback now always executes. Consider gating the pop only when the field is present, or relying on `exclude_defaults`/field-level exclusion to avoid the per-chunk Python hook.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming chat completion throughput benchmark (many short chunks, no tool calls): compare tokens/sec and per-chunk serialization latency with vs without the DeltaMessage._serialize hook.

findings.claim: In the streaming path, `_build_tool_maps(request.tools)` is called on every streaming invocation from within `_process_complete_block_streaming` and `_process_partial_block_streaming` (and `extract_tool_calls_streaming` may call them per matched block). `extract_tool_calls_streaming` is invoked once per generated token/delta, and `_build_tool_maps` iterates over all tools, builds `name_to_tool`, `name_to_allowed_props` (set(props.keys())), and `name_to_required` (set(required)) fresh each time. This request-invariant work is rebuilt on every token instead of being computed once and cached (e.g., keyed on request.tools identity or memoized on stream reset in `_reset_stream_state`). Cost scales with number of tools x tokens. Matches the redundant-hot-path-work pattern (per-iteration work that should be one-time setup).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming tool-call decode with a request carrying many (e.g., 20-50) tool definitions; measure per-token overhead / decode throughput vs Hermes parser baseline.

findings.claim: The streaming path re-runs `_FUNC_BLOCK_REGEX.finditer(current_text)` and `_normalize_model_output(current_text)` over the ENTIRE accumulated `current_text` on every token delta. As generation grows, this is O(N) per token -> O(N^2) total regex scanning / string replacement across the stream. The `_processed_len` cursor skips already-emitted blocks but the regex still scans the full text each call. Hermes uses lightweight `str.find` from a position cursor rather than a full-text DOTALL regex re-scan; the DOTALL `_FUNC_BLOCK_REGEX` here is more expensive per re-scan.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Stream a long tool-call output (few KB) and measure cumulative parser time; compare O(N^2) growth vs Hermes cursor-based find.

findings.claim: In `run_output_dispatcher_loop`, every RequestBatch that carries `scheduler_stats` (which the engine emits per scheduler step, i.e. on the hot per-step output path) now unconditionally calls `inner.lora_adapter_states()`. That acquires the `request_reg` mutex and iterates over ALL currently tracked requests, `.clone()`-ing each LoRA adapter name String into two freshly-allocated BTreeSets — even when no request uses LoRA at all. For a non-LoRA deployment this is pure waste: the sets are always empty yet the full-registry scan (and a second mutex acquisition separate from the earlier `apply_scheduler_stats`/`take_senders_for_outputs` locks in the same iteration) runs every step. The cost is O(active_requests) per step with String clones for LoRA users. Consider gating the snapshot on whether any LoRA request is currently tracked (e.g. a counter maintained on register/remove) and/or folding it into an existing registry lock acquisition in the same loop iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Steady-state token-generation throughput / dispatcher CPU with many concurrent active requests and no LoRA (empty-set path) vs. with LoRA adapters, measuring per-step overhead of lora_adapter_states().

In get_computed_blocks() (per-request scheduler hot path, called from Scheduler.schedule() for each request considered), the new block adds a per-group loop that calls block_pool.emit_cached_block_events() for every prefix-cache hit. emit_cached_block_events() calls resolve_block_hashes() and then, inside a loop over num_cached_blocks, invokes maybe_convert_block_hash() and generate_block_hash_extra_keys() for EVERY reused prefix block on EVERY scheduling of the request. This re-computes external hashes and extra-keys for the entire cached prefix (potentially hundreds of blocks for long-context reuse) each time the request enters get_computed_blocks, including on every re-admission/preemption resume. This is redundant O(num_cached_blocks) work on a hot path. Although gated by enable_kv_cache_events AND kv_cache_report_mode=='full', when active it repeats each iteration.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark scheduler throughput / get_computed_blocks latency with enable_kv_cache_events=True and kv_cache_report_mode='full' on a workload with high prefix-cache hit rate and long shared prefixes (e.g. 8k-token shared system prompt, many requests), comparing against report_mode='incremental'.

findings.claim: emit_cached_block_events() builds token_ids slice via request.all_token_ids[start_token_idx:end_token_idx] for the whole cached prefix (num_cached_blocks*block_size tokens) and copies it into the BlockStored event on every hit. For long reused prefixes this allocates/copies a large token list on the scheduler path each time a full-mode report is emitted, in addition to the per-block extra_keys list. This is a copy proportional to the entire reused prefix length rather than only newly stored blocks.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-request event-build overhead in emit_cached_block_events for varying reused-prefix lengths (1 vs 100+ blocks).

In DeepSeekV4Parser._convert_args and DeepSeekV32Parser._convert_args, the per-call argument converter does `func_name = next((s.name for s in self._tool_slots if s.args == raw_args), None)`. This runs on every streaming ARG_VALUE_CHUNK tick (a hot path). For each tick it linearly scans all `_tool_slots` and, via the `args` property, may materialize (`"".join(self._args_parts)`) the full accumulated argument string of each slot and compare it to `raw_args`. During streaming this becomes O(n_slots * arg_length) work per delta, i.e. effectively O(total_output^2) over the course of a long parallel-tool-call generation. The old DeepSeekV32ToolParser tracked the active tool index/name directly (`self._active_tool_index`, `self._active_tool_name`) rather than reverse-mapping args string to name each tick.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Streaming parse of a long tool-call argument (e.g. 8-16KB JSON string arg) and parallel tool calls; measure parse_delta latency per tick vs argument length.

findings.claim: deepseek_v4.py defines `_PARTIAL_PARAM_RE` with `(.*)$` under re.DOTALL and calls `_PARTIAL_PARAM_RE.search(raw_args, last_end)` on every partial streaming tick inside `_dsml_arg_converter`. Combined with `_PARAM_RE.finditer(raw_args)` re-scanning the *entire* accumulated `raw_args` on each tick, argument parsing re-parses the whole buffer every delta -> O(output^2). This is a milder analog of the historical spin/ReDoS pattern: not adversarial-backtracking per se (the greedy `.*` is anchored), but the full-buffer re-scan per streaming tick is a real quadratic cost. Verify raw_args is the whole accumulated arg string (it is, via ToolCallSlot.args) rather than an incremental delta.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Feed incrementally growing raw_args (partial=True) of increasing length; measure cumulative _dsml_arg_converter time to confirm quadratic growth.
